---
title: "Self-Medicating Social Pain? Bullying and Non-Medical Prescription Opioid Use among Adolescents — Reproducible Code"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
---

<!-- 
  This script contains all analysis code for:
  "Self-Medicating Social Pain? Bullying and Non-Medical Prescription
   Opioid Use among Adolescents"
  # MODIFIED IN v3 — comment header updated to match Manuscript_v_final_v4.Rmd

  Generated from nonmedicaluse_painmed_v44.Rmd (Reviewer41 instructions).
  Code only — no manuscript prose. All chunks documented with didactic comments.

  Replicability: All outputs verified against Manuscript_v_final_v4.Rmd and
  SupplementaryMaterial_v_final_v4.Rmd. See verification notes at end of script.
  # MODIFIED IN v3 — verification references updated to _v4 ground truth documents
-->

```{r setup, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 1: SETUP
# Purpose: Load all required R packages and set global options for reproducible analysis.
# Notes: All subsequent chunks depend on these packages. Session info is printed at end of script.
# ============================================================

library(RODBC)      # Connects R to Microsoft Access databases via ODBC
library(dplyr)      # Provides data manipulation verbs: mutate, filter, select, etc.
library(survey)     # Implements survey-weighted analyses: svydesign, svyglm, svymean
library(psych)      # Provides tetrachoric() for tetrachoric correlations and alpha
library(lavaan)     # Fits confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) with WLSMV estimator
library(knitr)      # Provides kable() for rendering tables in R Markdown output
library(ggplot2)    # Creates publication-quality figures (forest plots, bar charts)
library(flextable)  # Creates Word-native formatted tables for docx output
library(officer)    # Supports Word document creation alongside flextable
library(here)       # Portable file paths relative to project root
library(patchwork)  # Combines multiple ggplot panels into composite figures
library(eulerr)     # Proportional Euler diagrams for set overlap visualization
library(marginaleffects)  # AMEs on probability scale (Allison delta fix)

# Set global chunk options: suppress all messages/warnings in output by default
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,       # Do not print R code in the rendered document

  message = FALSE,    # Suppress package loading messages
  warning = FALSE,    # Suppress warnings (e.g., survey lonely PSU)
  comment = "#>"      # Prefix for printed R output
)

# Register Times New Roman as the serif font for ggplot2 on Windows
if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") {
  grDevices::windowsFonts(serif = grDevices::windowsFont("Times New Roman"))
}

# Handle strata with a single primary sampling unit gracefully
# Without this, svyglm() would error when a stratum has only one PSU
options(survey.lonely.psu = "adjust")
```

```{r data-load, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 2: DATA LOADING
# Purpose: Connect to the 2023 YRBS Access database and extract the analysis dataset (N = 20,103).
# Notes: Requires RODBC and a local copy of 2023_XXH_YRBS_Data.mdb in data/. Three-stage cluster design: counties > schools > classrooms.
# ============================================================

# Connect to the 2023 YRBS Access database in the project data/ folder.
# The here package resolves the path relative to the project root (.here file).
# See README.md for instructions on obtaining the data from the CDC.
db_path <- here::here("data", "2023_XXH_YRBS_Data")
conn <- odbcConnectAccess2007(db_path)

# Read the XXHq table, which contains the national combined dataset (N = 20,103)
yrbs <- sqlFetch(conn, "XXHq")

# Close the database connection immediately after reading to free resources
close(conn)

# ─── DATA INTEGRITY CHECKS ───
stopifnot("Expected ~20,103 rows" = nrow(yrbs) >= 19000 & nrow(yrbs) <= 21000)
stopifnot("Required columns present" = all(c("psu", "stratum", "weight",
  "q2", "q3", "raceeth", "q49", "q31", "q41", "q46", "q50",
  "q24", "q25", "q27", "q28", "q29", "q19", "q57") %in% names(yrbs)))
stopifnot("No duplicate rows" = !any(duplicated(yrbs)))
stopifnot("All weights positive" = all(yrbs$weight > 0, na.rm = TRUE))
```

```{r recode-outcome-demographics, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 3: RECODE OUTCOME AND DEMOGRAPHICS
# Purpose: Create analysis-ready factor variables for the primary outcome (Q49, lifetime NMPOU), secondary outcome (Q92, past-30-day NMPOU), and demographic covariates (sex, race/ethnicity, grade).
# Notes: Race/ethnicity uses the CDC composite variable `raceeth` (derived from Q4 Hispanic + Q5 race multi-select). All factors use the reference category as the first level for odds ratio interpretation.
# ============================================================

# MAIN ANALYSIS variables

yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(

  # ─── PRIMARY OUTCOME: Q49 — Lifetime NMPOU ───
  # Q49: "How many times have you taken prescription pain medicine without
  #       a doctor's prescription or differently than how a doctor told
  #       you to use it?"
  # Q49 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2 = "1 or 2 times", ... 6 = "40+ times"
  # Dichotomize: 1 → No (never used), 2-7 → Yes (any use)
  q49_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q49 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → no lifetime NMPOU
      q49 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any frequency → lifetime NMPOU endorsed
      TRUE ~ NA_character_         # Missing or out-of-range → NA
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")        # "No" is reference for OR interpretation
  ),

  # ─── SECONDARY OUTCOME: Q92 — Past-30-Day NMPOU ───
  # Q92: "During the past 30 days, how many times did you take prescription
  #       pain medicine without a doctor's prescription or differently than
  #       how a doctor told you to use it?"
  # Q92 is a NATIONAL-ONLY item → ~38% structural missingness
  # Q92 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  q92_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q92 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → no past-30-day NMPOU
      q92 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → past-30-day NMPOU
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ─── SEX: Q2 ───
  # Q2 codebook: 1 = Female, 2 = Male
  # Male is set as reference so AOR for Female tests sex difference
  q2_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q2 == 1 ~ "Female",         # Q2 = 1 → Female
      q2 == 2 ~ "Male",           # Q2 = 2 → Male
      TRUE ~ NA_character_         # Missing → NA
    ),
    levels = c("Male", "Female")   # Male = reference category
  ),

  # ─── RACE/ETHNICITY: from CDC composite variable `raceeth` ───
  # The CDC constructs `raceeth` from Q4 (Hispanic Y/N) and Q5 (race,
  # multi-select letter codes A-H). Hispanic/Latino ethnicity takes
  # precedence: Q4=Yes → Latine regardless of race response.
  # raceeth codebook: 1=AI/AN, 2=Asian, 3=Black, 4=NH/PI, 5=White,
  #   6=Hispanic/Latino, 7=Multiple-Hispanic, 8=Multiple-Non-Hispanic
  # Collapsed into 5 categories for adequate cell sizes.
  # NOTE: Raw Q5 uses multi-character codes (e.g., "B", "CE", "ABCDE")
  # for multi-race selection, making direct reconstruction impractical.
  # The CDC `raceeth` variable applies the standard precedence algorithm.
  q4_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      raceeth %in% c(6, 7) ~ "Latine/Multiple Latine", # Hispanic/Latino (any race)
      raceeth %in% c(1, 2, 4) ~ "Other",               # Non-Hispanic: AI/AN, Asian, NH/PI
      raceeth == 3 ~ "Black",                           # Non-Hispanic Black
      raceeth == 5 ~ "White",                           # Non-Hispanic White (reference)
      raceeth == 8 ~ "Multiple Non-Latine",             # Non-Hispanic Multiple
      TRUE ~ NA_character_                               # Missing → NA
    ),
    levels = c("White", "Other", "Black",
               "Latine/Multiple Latine", "Multiple Non-Latine")
  ),

  # ─── GRADE: Q3 ───
  # Q3 codebook: 1 = 9th, 2 = 10th, 3 = 11th, 4 = 12th
  # Used instead of age because Cramer's V = 0.61 (near-collinear)
  q3_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q3 == 1 ~ "9th",            # 9th grade
      q3 == 2 ~ "10th",           # 10th grade
      q3 == 3 ~ "11th",           # 11th grade
      q3 == 4 ~ "12th",           # 12th grade
      TRUE ~ NA_character_         # Missing → NA
    ),
    levels = c("9th", "10th", "11th", "12th")  # 9th = reference
  )
)

# Verify q4_1 was constructed correctly (should have 5 non-empty levels)
cat("q4_1 distribution:\n")
print(table(yrbs$q4_1, useNA = "always"))
```

```{r recode-f1, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 4: RECODE FRAMEWORK 1 — GENERALIZED RISK-TAKING
# Purpose: Create binary factor variables for substance use (lifetime and past-30-day), risk behavior, and cocaine. Lifetime items form the su_life composite; past-30-day items form su_1 (sensitivity SM6).
# Notes: Order follows Figure 2: Alcohol > Marijuana > Cigarettes > Cocaine. For each substance: ever-use first, then past-30-day version. Temporal consistency: lifetime predictors match the lifetime outcome (Jessor, 1991).
# ============================================================

# MAIN ANALYSIS variables (lifetime) and SENSITIVITY variables (past-30-day)
# Order follows Figure 2: Alcohol > Marijuana > Cigarettes > Cocaine

yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # ALCOHOL
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q41: "During your life, on how many days have you had at least one drink?"
  # Q41 codebook: 1 = "0 days", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  # MAIN ANALYSIS — component of su_life composite
  q41_ever = factor(
    case_when(
      q41 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 days → never used alcohol
      q41 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any days → lifetime alcohol use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q42: Past-30-day alcohol use
  # Q42 codebook: 1 = "0 days", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  # SENSITIVITY — component of su_1 composite (SM6)
  q42_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q42 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 days → no recent alcohol use
      q42 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any days → past-30-day alcohol use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # MARIJUANA
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q46: "During your life, how many times have you used marijuana?"
  # Q46 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  # MAIN ANALYSIS — component of su_life composite
  q46_life = factor(
    case_when(
      q46 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → never used marijuana
      q46 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → lifetime marijuana use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q48: Past-30-day marijuana use
  # Q48 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-6 = various frequency categories
  # SENSITIVITY — component of su_1 composite (SM6)
  q48_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q48 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → no recent marijuana use
      q48 %in% 2:6 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → past-30-day marijuana use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # CIGARETTES
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q31: "Have you ever tried cigarette smoking, even one or two puffs?"
  # Q31 codebook: 1 = Yes (ever tried), 2 = No (never tried)
  # NOTE: Q31 coding is reversed from most items (1 = Yes, not 1 = No)
  # MAIN ANALYSIS — component of su_life composite
  q31_life = factor(
    case_when(
      q31 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Ever tried cigarettes → Yes
      q31 == 2 ~ "No",            # Never tried → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q33: Past-30-day cigarette smoking
  # Q33 codebook: 1 = "0 days", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  # SENSITIVITY — component of su_1 composite (SM6)
  q33_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q33 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 days → no recent cigarette use
      q33 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any days → past-30-day cigarette use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # COCAINE (drug-specific replication only — not a composite component)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q50: "During your life, how many times have you used any form of cocaine?"
  # Q50 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-7 = various frequency categories
  # Used as an outcome in the stacked-outcome specificity test (Chunk 14)
  q50_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q50 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → never used cocaine
      q50 %in% 2:7 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → lifetime cocaine use
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # RISK BEHAVIOR ITEMS (for rb_1 composite)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q10: "During the past 30 days, how many times did you ride in a car
  #       driven by someone who had been drinking alcohol?"
  # Q10 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-6 = various frequency categories
  # SENSITIVITY — included in rb_1_3item (SM7) but NOT in primary rb_1
  # Excluded because it captures exposure to others' substance use
  # rather than the adolescent's own risk behavior
  q10_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q10 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → did not ride with drunk driver
      q10 %in% 2:6 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → rode with drunk driver
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q12: "During the past 30 days, on how many days did you carry a weapon?"
  # Q12 codebook: 1 = "0 days", 2-5 = various frequency categories
  # MAIN ANALYSIS — component of rb_1 composite
  q12_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q12 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 days → did not carry a weapon
      q12 %in% 2:5 ~ "Yes",       # Any days → carried a weapon
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q17: "During the past 12 months, how many times were you in a physical fight?"
  # Q17 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-8 = various frequency categories
  # MAIN ANALYSIS — component of rb_1 composite
  q17_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q17 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → not in a physical fight
      q17 %in% 2:8 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → was in a physical fight
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  )
)
```

```{r recode-f2, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 5: RECODE FRAMEWORK 2 — DISTRESS RELIEF
# Purpose: Create binary factor variables for bullying victimization and suicidality, the two F2 composites hypothesized to show opioid-specific associations via social pain overlap mechanisms.
# Notes: Q24/Q25 (bullying) and Q27/Q28 (ideation/plan) use 1=Yes, 2=No (non-standard). Q29 (attempt) uses 1="0 times" (standard). Dating violence items (Q21/Q22) are in recode-f3 as a sensitivity analysis variable.
# ============================================================

# MAIN ANALYSIS: Bullying (bu_1) and Suicidality (sc_1)
# SENSITIVITY: Sadness (Q26) for SM1

yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # BULLYING ITEMS (for bu_1 composite) — MAIN ANALYSIS
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q24: "During the past 12 months, have you ever been bullied on school property?"
  # Q24 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q24_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q24 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Bullied on school property
      q24 == 2 ~ "No",            # Not bullied on school property
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q25: "During the past 12 months, have you ever been electronically bullied?"
  # Q25 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q25_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q25 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Electronically bullied
      q25 == 2 ~ "No",            # Not electronically bullied
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # SUICIDALITY ITEMS (for sc_1 composite) — MAIN ANALYSIS
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q27: "During the past 12 months, did you ever seriously consider
  #       attempting suicide?"
  # Q27 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q27_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q27 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Seriously considered suicide
      q27 == 2 ~ "No",            # Did not seriously consider
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q28: "During the past 12 months, did you make a plan about how
  #       you would attempt suicide?"
  # Q28 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q28_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q28 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Made a suicide plan
      q28 == 2 ~ "No",            # Did not make a plan
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q29: "During the past 12 months, how many times did you actually
  #       attempt suicide?"
  # Q29 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2 = "1 time", 3 = "2-3 times",
  #               4 = "4-5 times", 5 = "6+ times"
  # NOTE: Unlike Q27/Q28, here 1 = No (standard coding) and 2+ = Yes
  q29_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q29 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 attempts
      q29 %in% 2:5 ~ "Yes",       # 1+ attempts
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # SADNESS — SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS (SM1)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q26: "During the past 12 months, did you ever feel so sad or hopeless
  #       almost every day for two weeks or more in a row that you stopped
  #       doing some usual activities?"
  # Q26 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  # Used in SM1: persistent sadness replaces suicidality composite
  q26_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q26 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Persistent sadness endorsed
      q26 == 2 ~ "No",            # Not endorsed
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  )
)
```

```{r recode-f3, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 6: RECODE FRAMEWORK 3 — CONTEXTUAL ADVERSITY
# Purpose: Create binary factor variables for sexual victimization, household adversity, community safety, and dating violence (sensitivity SM2). Household/community items are national-module only (~30-34% structural missingness).
# Notes: sv_1 was reclassified from F2 to F3 in v32 based on 6 empirical tests (Table S8). Dating violence (dv_1) is included here as a sensitivity analysis variable for SM2.
# ============================================================

# Purpose: Create binary factor variables for sexual victimization,
#          household adversity, and community safety items.
#          Household/community items are NATIONAL-ONLY items with
#          ~30-34% structural missingness (administered only on the national
#          questionnaire module, not the standard state version).
#
# IMPORTANT CODING NOTES:
#   Q100, Q101: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (non-standard)
#   Q18: 1 = Yes (felt unsafe), 2 = No (non-standard)
#   Q91: 1 = "0 times" (No), 2-5 = frequencies (Yes) (standard)
#   Q102: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (for sensitivity S4)

yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(

  # ─── HOUSEHOLD ADVERSITY ITEMS (for hh_1 composite) ───

  # Q91: "During the past 12 months, how many times did you see a parent
  #       or other adult in your home hit, beat, kick, or physically hurt
  #       another adult in your home?"
  # Q91 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-5 = various frequency categories
  q91_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q91 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → no witnessed parental IPV
      q91 %in% 2:5 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → witnessed parental IPV
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q100: "Have you ever lived with someone who was a problem drinker or
  #        alcoholic, or who used illegal street drugs or abused Rx meds?"
  # Q100 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q100_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q100 == 1 ~ "Yes",          # Household substance use → Yes
      q100 == 2 ~ "No",           # No household substance use → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q101: "Have you ever lived with someone who was depressed, mentally
  #        ill, or suicidal?"
  # Q101 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q101_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q101 == 1 ~ "Yes",          # Household mental illness → Yes
      q101 == 2 ~ "No",           # No household mental illness → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q102: "Have you ever lived with a parent or other adult who served
  #        time in jail or prison?" (for sensitivity S4: hh_2 = hh_1 + Q102)
  # Q102 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No
  q102_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q102 == 1 ~ "Yes",          # Parental incarceration → Yes
      q102 == 2 ~ "No",           # No parental incarceration → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ─── COMMUNITY SAFETY ITEMS (for ms_1 composite) ───

  # Q14: "During the past 30 days, on how many days did you not go to
  #       school because you felt you would be unsafe at school or on
  #       your way to or from school?"
  # Q14 codebook: 1 = "0 days", 2-5 = various frequency categories
  q14_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q14 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 days → did not miss school for safety
      q14 %in% 2:5 ~ "Yes",       # Any days → missed school due to safety
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q15: "During the past 12 months, how many times has someone threatened
  #       or injured you with a weapon such as a gun, knife, or club on
  #       school property?"
  # Q15 codebook: 1 = "0 times", 2-8 = various frequency categories
  q15_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q15 == 1 ~ "No",            # 0 times → not weapon-threatened at school
      q15 %in% 2:8 ~ "Yes",       # Any times → weapon-threatened at school
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q18: "During the past 30 days, how often did you feel unsafe in
  #       your neighborhood?"
  # Q18 codebook: 1 = "Most of the time or always" (Yes),
  #               2 = "Never or rarely" (No)
  # NOTE: 1 = Yes is non-standard
  q18_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q18 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Felt unsafe in neighborhood → Yes
      q18 == 2 ~ "No",            # Did not feel unsafe → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # ─── SEXUAL VICTIMIZATION ITEMS (sv_1 — classified as F3 in v32) ───
  # Moved from recode-f2 to recode-f3 in v34 because sv_1 is an F3 variable.
  # Q19 and Q57 do not specify perpetrator identity, preventing attribution
  # to peer-based social pain (F2) vs. other sources of trauma (F3).

  # Q19: "Have you ever been physically forced to have sexual intercourse
  #       when you did not want to?"
  # Q19 codebook: 1 = Yes, 2 = No (NON-STANDARD: 1 = Yes)
  q19_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q19 == 1 ~ "Yes",           # Forced sexual intercourse → Yes
      q19 == 2 ~ "No",            # Not forced → No
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q57: Age at first sexual intercourse — used in two ways:
  #   (a) q57_12: binary indicator for age ≤12 (component of sv_1)
  #   (b) q57_5: three-level factor for sensitivity S5

  # Q57 codebook: 1 = "I have never had sexual intercourse",
  #   2 = "11 years old or younger", 3 = "12 years old",
  #   4 = "13 years old", ... 8 = "17 years old or older"

  # (a) Binary: age ≤12 vs. never/13+
  # Early debut at age 12 or younger is a marker of child sexual
  # victimization, not precocity (Finer and Philbin, 2013)
  q57_12 = factor(
    case_when(
      q57 %in% 2:3 ~ "Yes",       # Age 11 or younger + age 12 → ≤12
      q57 == 1 ~ "No",            # Never had sex → No
      q57 %in% 4:8 ~ "No",        # Age 13+ → No (not early debut)
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # (b) Three-level: for sensitivity S5 (tests early debut separately)
  q57_5 = factor(
    case_when(
      q57 == 1 ~ "No",            # Never had sex (reference)
      q57 %in% 2:3 ~ "12 or less",  # Age ≤12 (likely sexual abuse)
      q57 %in% 4:8 ~ "13 or more",  # Age 13+ (normative debut timing)
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "12 or less", "13 or more")
  ),

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # DATING VIOLENCE ITEMS (for dv_1 composite) — SENSITIVITY (SM2)
  # Moved from recode-f2 because dating violence is a sensitivity
  # analysis variable for sexual victimization, not a primary F2 variable.
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  # Q21: "During the past 12 months, how many times did someone you were
  #       dating or going out with physically hurt you on purpose?"
  # Q21 codebook: 1 = "I did not date", 2 = "0 times", 3-6 = frequencies
  # Coding: 1-2 → No (non-daters + daters without DV), 3+ → Yes (DV)
  q21_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q21 %in% 1:2 ~ "No",        # Did not date OR 0 times (no DV)
      q21 %in% 3:6 ~ "Yes",       # 1+ times → physical DV endorsed
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  ),

  # Q22: "During the past 12 months, how many times did someone you were
  #       dating or going out with force you to do sexual things?"
  # Q22 codebook: same structure as Q21
  q22_1 = factor(
    case_when(
      q22 %in% 1:2 ~ "No",        # Did not date OR 0 times (no sexual DV)
      q22 %in% 3:6 ~ "Yes",       # 1+ times → sexual DV endorsed
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    levels = c("No", "Yes")
  )
)
```

```{r composites, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 7: COMPOSITE CONSTRUCTION
# Purpose: Build all composite predictors from recoded items using the "any endorsed" rule. Also constructs leave-one-out (LOO) polysubstance composites for the stacked-outcome specificity test.
# Notes: Each composite: "Yes" if ANY item endorsed, "No" if ALL "No", NA if ANY missing. LOO composites exclude the target substance to avoid circularity in stacked models (Allison, 1999).
# ============================================================

yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(

  # ─── F1: LIFETIME POLYSUBSTANCE (su_life) ───
  su_life = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q31_life) | is.na(q41_ever) | is.na(q46_life) ~ NA_character_,
    q31_life == "No" & q41_ever == "No" & q46_life == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS — SM10: 4-substance polysubstance including cocaine ───
  # ADDED IN v4 — SM10 four-substance composite
  # Extends su_life (cigarettes + alcohol + marijuana) by adding cocaine (q50_1)
  su_life_coc = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q31_life) | is.na(q41_ever) | is.na(q46_life) | is.na(q50_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q31_life == "No" & q41_ever == "No" & q46_life == "No" & q50_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F1: PAST-30-DAY POLYSUBSTANCE (su_1) — sensitivity S6 ───
  su_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q33_1) | is.na(q42_1) | is.na(q48_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q33_1 == "No" & q42_1 == "No" & q48_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F1: RISK BEHAVIOR (rb_1) — 2-item: weapon carrying + fighting ───
  rb_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q12_1) | is.na(q17_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q12_1 == "No" & q17_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F1: THREE-ITEM RISK BEHAVIOR (rb_1_3item) — sensitivity S7 ───
  rb_1_3item = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q10_1) | is.na(q12_1) | is.na(q17_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q10_1 == "No" & q12_1 == "No" & q17_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F2: BULLYING (bu_1) ───
  bu_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q24_1) | is.na(q25_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q24_1 == "No" & q25_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F2: SUICIDALITY (sc_1) ───
  sc_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q27_1) | is.na(q28_1) | is.na(q29_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q27_1 == "No" & q28_1 == "No" & q29_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F3: SEXUAL VICTIMIZATION (sv_1) — moved from F2 to F3 in v32 ───
  sv_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q19_1) | is.na(q57_12) ~ NA_character_,
    q19_1 == "No" & q57_12 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F3: DATING VIOLENCE (dv_1) — sensitivity SM2 ───
  dv_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q21_1) | is.na(q22_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q21_1 == "No" & q22_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F3: HOUSEHOLD ADVERSITY (hh_1) ───
  hh_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q91_1) | is.na(q100_1) | is.na(q101_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q91_1 == "No" & q100_1 == "No" & q101_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F3: EXPANDED HOUSEHOLD ADVERSITY (hh_2) — sensitivity S4 ───
  hh_2 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q91_1) | is.na(q100_1) | is.na(q101_1) | is.na(q102_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q91_1 == "No" & q100_1 == "No" & q101_1 == "No" & q102_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),

  # ─── F3: COMMUNITY SAFETY ADVERSITY (ms_1) ───
  ms_1 = factor(case_when(
    is.na(q14_1) | is.na(q15_1) | is.na(q18_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q14_1 == "No" & q15_1 == "No" & q18_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes"))
)

# ─── LEAVE-ONE-OUT (LOO) POLYSUBSTANCE COMPOSITES ───
# For drug-specific replications: each outcome substance is excluded
# from its own polysubstance composite, with NMPOU substituted in.
yrbs <- yrbs %>% mutate(
  su_loo_nmpou = su_life,          # NMPOU row: standard su_life
  su_loo_alc = factor(case_when(   # Alcohol row: cigs + marijuana + NMPOU
    is.na(q31_life) | is.na(q46_life) | is.na(q49_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q31_life == "No" & q46_life == "No" & q49_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),
  su_loo_mar = factor(case_when(   # Marijuana row: cigs + alcohol + NMPOU
    is.na(q31_life) | is.na(q41_ever) | is.na(q49_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q31_life == "No" & q41_ever == "No" & q49_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),
  su_loo_cig = factor(case_when(   # Cigarettes row: alcohol + marijuana + NMPOU
    is.na(q41_ever) | is.na(q46_life) | is.na(q49_1) ~ NA_character_,
    q41_ever == "No" & q46_life == "No" & q49_1 == "No" ~ "No",
    TRUE ~ "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes")),
  su_loo_coc = su_life             # Cocaine row: standard su_life (cocaine not in triad)
)
```

```{r reliability, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 8: COMPOSITE RELIABILITY
# Purpose: Compute internal consistency for each composite index. Uses tetrachoric correlations (appropriate for binary items), Spearman-Brown for 2-item composites, and alpha + omega (CFA/WLSMV) for 3+ items.
# Notes: Bootstrap CIs (B=500) provide uncertainty quantification. Nunnally & Bernstein (1994) provide the psychometric framework. Heywood cases (loadings > 1) are flagged as NA.
# ============================================================

compute_reliability <- function(df, items, label, B = 500) {
  # Convert factor items to 0/1 numeric, keep complete cases only
  mat <- df %>%
    mutate(across(all_of(items), ~ as.numeric(.x == "Yes"))) %>%
    select(all_of(items)) %>%
    na.omit()
  n_complete <- nrow(mat)

  # Helper: compute point estimate from a matrix
  .sb_from_mat <- function(m) {
    tc <- tryCatch(tetrachoric(m), error = function(e) NULL)
    if (is.null(tc)) return(NA)
    r_tet <- tc$rho[1, 2]
    (2 * r_tet) / (1 + r_tet)
  }
  .alpha_from_mat <- function(m) {
    tc <- tryCatch(tetrachoric(m), error = function(e) NULL)
    if (is.null(tc)) return(NA)
    k <- ncol(tc$rho)
    r_bar <- (sum(tc$rho) - k) / (k * (k - 1))
    (k * r_bar) / (1 + (k - 1) * r_bar)
  }

  # Edge-case guards
  if (length(items) < 2) return(list(label = label, n = n_complete, alpha = NA,
    omega = NA, sb = NA, r_tet = NA, loadings = NA, ci_lo = NA, ci_hi = NA))
  if (n_complete < 30) return(list(label = label, n = n_complete, alpha = NA,
    omega = NA, sb = NA, r_tet = NA, loadings = NA, ci_lo = NA, ci_hi = NA))

  # Bootstrap CIs for primary metric (reproducible)
  set.seed(20260225)
  boot_vals <- replicate(B, {
    idx <- sample(n_complete, n_complete, replace = TRUE)
    boot_mat <- mat[idx, , drop = FALSE]
    if (length(items) == 2) .sb_from_mat(boot_mat)
    else .alpha_from_mat(boot_mat)
  })
  boot_vals <- boot_vals[!is.na(boot_vals)]
  ci_lo <- if (length(boot_vals) >= 20) round(quantile(boot_vals, 0.025), 2) else NA
  ci_hi <- if (length(boot_vals) >= 20) round(quantile(boot_vals, 0.975), 2) else NA

  if (length(items) == 2) {
    # Two-item composite: use tetrachoric r and Spearman-Brown
    tc <- tetrachoric(mat)
    r_tet <- tc$rho[1, 2]
    sb <- (2 * r_tet) / (1 + r_tet)
    return(list(label = label, n = n_complete, alpha = NA, omega = NA,
                sb = round(sb, 2), r_tet = round(r_tet, 2), loadings = NA,
                ci_lo = ci_lo, ci_hi = ci_hi))
  }

  # 3+ items: tetrachoric alpha
  tc <- tetrachoric(mat)
  k <- ncol(tc$rho)
  r_bar <- (sum(tc$rho) - k) / (k * (k - 1))
  alpha_val <- (k * r_bar) / (1 + (k - 1) * r_bar)

  # Pairwise tetrachoric correlations (for formative indices)
  pairwise_r <- tc$rho[lower.tri(tc$rho)]

  # McDonald's omega via one-factor CFA with WLSMV
  omega_val <- NA; loadings_val <- NA
  mod <- paste0("f =~ ", paste(items, collapse = " + "))
  mat_ord <- mat
  for (v in names(mat_ord)) mat_ord[[v]] <- ordered(mat_ord[[v]])
  tryCatch({
    fit <- cfa(mod, data = mat_ord, estimator = "WLSMV")
    sl <- standardizedSolution(fit)
    lambdas <- sl$est.std[sl$op == "=~"]
    if (any(lambdas > 1) || any(lambdas < 0)) {
      omega_val <- NA              # Heywood case
    } else {
      omega_val <- sum(lambdas)^2 / (sum(lambdas)^2 + sum(1 - lambdas^2))
    }
    loadings_val <- round(lambdas, 2)
  }, error = function(e) {})

  return(list(label = label, n = n_complete,
              alpha = round(alpha_val, 2),
              omega = ifelse(is.na(omega_val), NA, round(omega_val, 2)),
              sb = NA, r_tet = NA, loadings = loadings_val,
              ci_lo = ci_lo, ci_hi = ci_hi,
              pairwise_r = round(pairwise_r, 2)))
}

# Compute reliability for all primary composites
rel_su_life <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q31_life", "q41_ever", "q46_life"), "su_life")
rel_rb_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q12_1", "q17_1"), "rb_1")
rel_rb_1_3item <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q10_1", "q12_1", "q17_1"), "rb_1_3item")
rel_bu_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q24_1", "q25_1"), "bu_1")
rel_sc_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q27_1", "q28_1", "q29_1"), "sc_1")
rel_sv_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q19_1", "q57_12"), "sv_1")
rel_hh_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q91_1", "q100_1", "q101_1"), "hh_1")
rel_ms_1    <- compute_reliability(yrbs, c("q14_1", "q15_1", "q18_1"), "ms_1")
all_rel <- list(rel_su_life, rel_rb_1, rel_bu_1, rel_sc_1, rel_sv_1, rel_hh_1, rel_ms_1)
```

```{r survey-design, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 9: SURVEY DESIGN AND SUBSAMPLE DEFINITIONS
# Purpose: Create survey design objects incorporating the YRBS three-stage cluster sampling design (PSUs, strata, weights) and define analytic subsamples.
# Notes: Two-sample strategy (Graham, 2009): full sample for models using standard items; national-module subsample for models requiring household/community items. See Lumley (2010), Complex Surveys: A Guide to Analysis Using R.
# ============================================================

# Two-sample strategy: full sample for M1/M2a/M3; subsample for M4/M5.
###############################################################################

# Define subsample flags BEFORE creating survey design
yrbs$in_subsample <- !is.na(yrbs$q91_1) | !is.na(yrbs$q100_1) | !is.na(yrbs$q101_1)
yrbs$is_dater <- (!is.na(yrbs$q21) & yrbs$q21 >= 2) | (!is.na(yrbs$q22) & yrbs$q22 >= 2)

# Full-sample survey design (N = 20,103)
des_full <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                      data = yrbs, nest = TRUE)
# National-module subsample
des_sub <- subset(des_full, in_subsample == TRUE)
# Daters subsample (for sensitivity S2)
des_daters_sub <- subset(des_full, in_subsample == TRUE & is_dater == TRUE)

# Store sample sizes
n_full <- nrow(yrbs)
n_sub <- sum(yrbs$in_subsample)

# ─── ANALYTIC SAMPLE FLOW ───
n_total_raw <- nrow(yrbs)
n_miss_outcome <- sum(is.na(yrbs$q49_1))
n_miss_sex <- sum(is.na(yrbs$q2_1))
n_miss_grade <- sum(is.na(yrbs$q3_1))
n_miss_race <- sum(is.na(yrbs$q4_1))
# Complete cases for M3 (full-sample model; sv_1 now enters at M4)
m3_vars <- c("q49_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1", "su_life", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1")
n_complete_m3 <- sum(complete.cases(yrbs[, m3_vars]))
# Complete cases for M5 (subsample model; includes sv_1 as F3)
m5_vars <- c(m3_vars, "sv_1", "hh_1", "ms_1")
n_complete_m5 <- sum(complete.cases(yrbs[yrbs$in_subsample, m5_vars]))
```

```{r helper-functions, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 10: HELPER FUNCTIONS
# Purpose: Define reusable functions for fitting survey-weighted logistic regressions and formatting output tables. These functions are called by all model-fitting chunks.
# Notes: fit_model() returns AOR with 95% CI. ft_out() creates publication-ready Word tables via flextable. Both enforce Times New Roman 10pt/12pt for Pediatrics compliance.
# ============================================================

# fit_model(): Fit survey-weighted logistic regression, return AOR table.
# compute_attenuation(): % change in log-odds beta between nested models.
###############################################################################

fit_model <- function(formula, design, label = "") {
  fit <- svyglm(formula, design = design, family = quasibinomial())
  cc <- summary(fit)$coefficients
  terms <- rownames(cc)[-1]
  results <- data.frame(
    term  = terms,
    beta  = cc[terms, "Estimate"],
    AOR   = round(exp(cc[terms, "Estimate"]), 2),
    CI_lo = round(exp(cc[terms, "Estimate"] - 1.96 * cc[terms, "Std. Error"]), 2),
    CI_hi = round(exp(cc[terms, "Estimate"] + 1.96 * cc[terms, "Std. Error"]), 2),
    p     = cc[terms, "Pr(>|t|)"],
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  results$sig <- ifelse(results$p < 0.001, "***",
                 ifelse(results$p < 0.01,  "**",
                 ifelse(results$p < 0.05,  "*", "")))
  attr(results, "model") <- fit
  attr(results, "n") <- nobs(fit)
  attr(results, "label") <- label
  attr(results, "converged") <- fit$converged
  return(results)
}

## METHODS NOTE: compute_attenuation() computes NAIVE percentage change in log-odds
## between nested models. Karlson et al. (2012) show this conflates two effects:
## (1) genuine confounding/mediation (the added variable explains some of X's effect),
## and (2) rescaling (adding Z changes residual variance, rescaling ALL coefficients).
## For the mediator-specific question (how much of X's effect goes through su_life?),
## use the KHB results in Table S11 instead. For the F3-addition question (how much
## does adding adversity variables attenuate distress associations?), this naive
## measure is the only one available because KHB is structured around a single mediator.
compute_attenuation <- function(res_earlier, res_later, term) {
  beta_e <- res_earlier$beta[res_earlier$term == term]
  beta_l <- res_later$beta[res_later$term == term]
  if (length(beta_e) == 0 || length(beta_l) == 0) return(NA)
  if (abs(beta_e) < 1e-10) return(NA)
  round(((beta_e - beta_l) / beta_e) * 100, 1)
}

# fmt_p(): Format p-values with max 4 decimal places (comment R5)
fmt_p <- function(p, digits = 4) {
  if (is.na(p)) return("NA")
  if (p < 10^(-digits)) return(paste0("< ", format(10^(-digits), scientific = FALSE)))
  formatC(p, format = "f", digits = digits)
}

# ft_out(): Convert a data frame to a flextable with 10pt body, bold headers,
# 12pt TNR caption, optional footer notes, and single-spacing
ft_out <- function(df, cap = "", align_spec = NULL, notes = NULL, spacing = 1.0) {
  ft <- flextable(df)
  ft <- fontsize(ft, size = 10, part = "all")
  ft <- font(ft, fontname = "Times New Roman", part = "all")
  ft <- bold(ft, part = "header")
  ft <- line_spacing(ft, space = spacing, part = "all")
  ft <- padding(ft, padding.top = 0, padding.bottom = 0, part = "all")
  if (!is.null(align_spec)) {
    for (k in seq_along(align_spec)) {
      a <- switch(align_spec[k], "l" = "left", "c" = "center", "r" = "right", "left")
      ft <- align(ft, j = k, align = a, part = "body")
    }
  }
  ft <- align(ft, align = "center", part = "header")
  ft <- set_caption(ft, caption = as_paragraph(
    as_chunk(cap, props = fp_text(font.size = 12, font.family = "Times New Roman", color = "black"))),
    fp_p = fp_par(text.align = "center"))
  if (!is.null(notes)) {
    ft <- add_footer_lines(ft, values = notes)
    ft <- fontsize(ft, size = 10, part = "footer")
    ft <- font(ft, fontname = "Times New Roman", part = "footer")
    ft <- line_spacing(ft, space = 1.0, part = "footer")
    ft <- align(ft, align = "justify", part = "footer")
  }
  ft <- autofit(ft)
  ft <- set_table_properties(ft, layout = "autofit", width = 1)
  ft
}

# fmt_aor_ci(): Format inline AOR with 95% CI from a model results data frame
# Returns "X.XX (95% CI: X.XX-X.XX)" for use in prose
fmt_aor_ci <- function(res, term) {
  row <- res[res$term == term, ]
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("--")
  paste0(sprintf("%.2f", row$AOR), " (95% CI: ",
         sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_lo), "-", sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_hi), ")")
}

# fmt_aor_inline(): Format AOR for use inside parenthetical prose
# Returns "AOR = X.XX, 95% CI: X.XX-X.XX" — avoids double parentheses
fmt_aor_inline <- function(res, term) {
  row <- res[res$term == term, ]
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("--")
  paste0("AOR = ", sprintf("%.2f", row$AOR), ", 95% CI: ",
         sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_lo), "-", sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_hi))
}
```

```{r set-a-models, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 11: SET A MODELS — TOTAL ASSOCIATION
# Purpose: Fit models M1 (demographics only), M2a (+F2 distress, full sample), and M2b (+F3 adversity, subsample) to estimate unconditional associations before adjusting for polysubstance use.
# Notes: Set A tests whether each framework is associated with NMPOU before controlling for generalized risk-taking (F1). M2a uses the full sample; M2b uses the national-module subsample.
# ============================================================

# These do NOT adjust for polysubstance involvement. They estimate the
# unconditional (total) association of each framework with NMPOU.
#
## METHODS NOTE: In KHB terms, M2a is the "reduced model" for distress
## variables -- it gives their total association with NMPOU before the
## mediator (polysubstance use) is introduced. Comparing M2a coefficients
## with M3/M5 coefficients NAIVELY conflates confounding with rescaling
## (Karlson et al. 2012, Eq. 7). The KHB decomposition in chunk 15
## corrects for this.
###############################################################################

# M1: Demographics only (baseline) — full sample
res_M1 <- fit_model(q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     des_full, "M1")

# M2a: Demographics + F2 Distress (bullying + suicidality) — full sample
res_M2a <- fit_model(q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + bu_1 + sc_1,
                      des_full, "M2a")

# M2b: Demographics + F3 Adversity (sexual vict. + household + community) — subsample
# NOTE: sv_1 is available on the full sample but runs on the subsample here
# for consistency with hh_1/ms_1 (subsample-only variables). This is standard
# practice: the model runs on the sample where all included predictors are observed.
res_M2b <- fit_model(q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
                      des_sub, "M2b")
```

```{r set-b-models, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 12: SET B MODELS — CONDITIONAL SPECIFICITY
# Purpose: Fit models M3 (+F1+F2, full sample), M4 (+sv_1+hh_1, subsample), and M5 (+ms_1, subsample) to test whether distress associations persist after controlling for polysubstance use and contextual adversity.
# Notes: M5 is the primary model (all 7 predictors). Block-wise Wald tests evaluate incremental improvement at each step. Subsample models require national-module items.
# ============================================================

# These adjust for polysubstance (su_life) and risk behavior (rb_1).
# The critical test: do distress associations PERSIST after F1 adjustment?
#
## METHODS NOTE: The M3 → M4 → M5 sequence adds variables progressively.
## In logistic regression, each addition changes the residual variance,
## rescaling ALL coefficients (Karlson et al. 2012). This means the change
## in bu_1's coefficient from M3 to M5 reflects BOTH (a) confounding by
## F3 variables and (b) rescaling from reduced residual variance. The
## compute_attenuation() results for M3-sub → M5 are therefore NAIVE
## estimates. The KHB-corrected mediation proportions (Table S11) address
## this for the su_life mediator specifically.
###############################################################################

# M3: F1 + F2 conditional — full sample (sv_1 now in F3, not here)
res_M3 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1,
  des_full, "M3")

# M3 diagnostic: same formula on subsample to validate comparability
res_M3_sub <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1,
  des_sub, "M3-sub")

# Dynamic max AOR change between full-sample M3 and subsample M3
val_terms <- c("q2_1Female",
               "q3_110th", "q3_111th", "q3_112th",
               "q4_1Black", "q4_1Latine/Multiple Latine",
               "q4_1Multiple Non-Latine", "q4_1Other",
               "su_lifeYes", "rb_1Yes", "bu_1Yes", "sc_1Yes")
max_aor_change <- max(sapply(val_terms, function(t) {
  a <- res_M3$AOR[res_M3$term == t]
  b <- res_M3_sub$AOR[res_M3_sub$term == t]
  abs(a - b) / a * 100
}))

# M4: M3 + sexual victimization + household adversity — subsample
# sv_1 enters here as F3 variable alongside hh_1
res_M4 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1,
  des_sub, "M4")

# M5: M4 + community safety adversity — subsample (FULL ECOLOGICAL MODEL)
res_M5 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5")

# Block-wise Wald tests for model comparison
# M3 vs M2a: joint significance of su_life + rb_1
wald_m3 <- regTermTest(attr(res_M3, "model"), ~ su_life + rb_1, method = "Wald")
# M4 vs M3-sub: joint significance of sv_1 + hh_1 (both F3)
wald_m4 <- regTermTest(attr(res_M4, "model"), ~ sv_1 + hh_1, method = "Wald")
# M5 vs M4: significance of ms_1
wald_m5 <- regTermTest(attr(res_M5, "model"), ~ ms_1, method = "Wald")
```

```{r drug-specific, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 13: DRUG-SPECIFIC MODELS
# Purpose: Fit M5-equivalent logistic regressions separately for each of five substances (NMPOU, alcohol, marijuana, cigarettes, cocaine) to compare predictor magnitudes across substance types.
# Notes: Each model uses leave-one-out polysubstance composites to avoid circularity. These are the basis for Table S6.
# ============================================================

##
## METHODS NOTE (Drug-specific LOO composites):
##
## Each substance model uses a "leave-one-out" (LOO) polysubstance composite
## that EXCLUDES the outcome substance from the polysubstance count. This is
## critical for two reasons:
##
## 1. AVOIDING MECHANICAL CORRELATION: If NMPOU were both the outcome AND part
##    of the polysubstance predictor, the model would have a near-tautological
##    association (using the outcome to predict itself). The LOO composite for
##    NMPOU (su_life) counts cigarettes + alcohol + marijuana; for alcohol
##    (su_loo_alc), it counts cigarettes + marijuana + NMPOU; etc.
##
## 2. COMPARABILITY ACROSS SUBSTANCES: Each model has the same structure
##    (same predictors, same subsample, same design), differing only in
##    the dependent variable and corresponding LOO composite. This makes
##    cross-substance AOR comparisons meaningful — differences in distress
##    associations reflect genuine substance specificity, not artifacts
##    of model specification.
##
## NOTE: Cocaine is an exception. It is not part of the original triad
## (cigarettes + alcohol + marijuana), so su_loo_coc = su_life (the full
## triad composite, which already excludes cocaine).
##
## These 5-substance AORs feed into Figure 2 (substance specificity plot)
## and the stacked-outcome heterogeneity test (CHUNK 14).
##

# NMPOU: identical to M5 above
res_ds_nmpou <- res_M5

# Alcohol: DV = lifetime alcohol (q41_ever), LOO = cigs + marijuana + NMPOU
res_ds_alc <- fit_model(
  q41_ever ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_loo_alc + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5-Alcohol")

# Marijuana: DV = lifetime marijuana (q46_life), LOO = cigs + alcohol + NMPOU
res_ds_mar <- fit_model(
  q46_life ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_loo_mar + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5-Marijuana")

# Cigarettes: DV = lifetime cigarettes (q31_life), LOO = alcohol + marijuana + NMPOU
res_ds_cig <- fit_model(
  q31_life ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_loo_cig + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5-Cigarettes")

# Cocaine: DV = lifetime cocaine (q50_1), LOO = su_life (cocaine not in triad)
res_ds_coc <- fit_model(
  q50_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_loo_coc + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5-Cocaine")
```

```{r stacked-model, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 14: STACKED-OUTCOME HETEROGENEITY TEST
# Purpose: Test whether predictor-substance associations differ significantly across five substance types using a stacked (long-format) dataset with predictor-by-substance interactions.
# Notes: Method: reshape data so each student appears 5 times (one per substance). LOO polysubstance composites prevent circularity. Weights divided by 5 to preserve effective N. Clustering at PSU + student_id accounts for within-person correlation. Joint Wald test on interaction terms evaluates opioid specificity. Reference: Allison (1999), Comparing logit and probit coefficients across groups.
# ============================================================

#
## METHODS NOTE (Allison 1999): Comparing logit coefficients across groups
## (here, substance types) is potentially invalid if residual variation
## differs across groups. Allison proposes estimating a residual-variance
## ratio (delta) and testing coefficients after adjusting for it.
##
## IDENTIFICATION CONSTRAINT: This model does NOT include interactions for
## su_loo or demographic controls (q2_1, q3_1, q4_1). Their coefficients
## are CONSTRAINED EQUAL across all five substance types. This is required
## for the interaction terms on the other variables to be identified and
## interpretable. The assumption is that polysubstance use and demographics
## have the same association with substance use regardless of which substance
## is the outcome.
##
## LIMITATION: This implementation uses standard logistic regression with
## interactions rather than Allison's heteroscedastic logit. It does NOT
## estimate delta (the residual-variance ratio between substance types).
## If residual variation differs substantially across substance outcomes,
## the interaction tests could be biased. The mixed pattern of results
## (bullying NMPOU-specific, sexual victimization stronger for other
## substances) argues against a pure delta explanation, but this cannot
## be formally tested without estimating delta.
##
## WEIGHT NORMALIZATION: Weights are divided by 5 (or 3) because each
## student contributes 5 (or 3) rows. Without this, the population total
## would be inflated 5-fold (Allison 1999).
##
## CLUSTERING: id = ~psu + student_id clusters at both the PSU level
## (survey design) and the student level (within-person correlation
## across the 5 stacked rows).
###############################################################################

# ─── BUILD 5-SUBSTANCE STACKED DATASET ───
sub_data <- yrbs[yrbs$in_subsample, ]         # Restrict to national-module subsample
sub_data$student_id <- seq_len(nrow(sub_data)) # Unique student ID for clustering

# Row 1: NMPOU outcome
r1 <- sub_data %>% transmute(
  student_id, psu, stratum, weight, outcome_type = "NMPOU",
  Y = q49_1, su_loo = su_loo_nmpou,
  q2_1, q3_1, q4_1, rb_1, bu_1, sc_1, sv_1, hh_1, ms_1)

# Row 2: Alcohol outcome
r2 <- sub_data %>% transmute(
  student_id, psu, stratum, weight, outcome_type = "Alcohol",
  Y = q41_ever, su_loo = su_loo_alc,
  q2_1, q3_1, q4_1, rb_1, bu_1, sc_1, sv_1, hh_1, ms_1)

# Row 3: Marijuana outcome
r3 <- sub_data %>% transmute(
  student_id, psu, stratum, weight, outcome_type = "Marijuana",
  Y = q46_life, su_loo = su_loo_mar,
  q2_1, q3_1, q4_1, rb_1, bu_1, sc_1, sv_1, hh_1, ms_1)

# Row 4: Cigarettes outcome
r4 <- sub_data %>% transmute(
  student_id, psu, stratum, weight, outcome_type = "Cigarettes",
  Y = q31_life, su_loo = su_loo_cig,
  q2_1, q3_1, q4_1, rb_1, bu_1, sc_1, sv_1, hh_1, ms_1)

# Row 5: Cocaine outcome
r5 <- sub_data %>% transmute(
  student_id, psu, stratum, weight, outcome_type = "Cocaine",
  Y = q50_1, su_loo = su_loo_coc,
  q2_1, q3_1, q4_1, rb_1, bu_1, sc_1, sv_1, hh_1, ms_1)

# Combine into stacked dataset (5 rows per student)
stacked5 <- bind_rows(r1, r2, r3, r4, r5)
stacked5$outcome_type <- factor(stacked5$outcome_type,
  levels = c("NMPOU", "Alcohol", "Marijuana", "Cigarettes", "Cocaine"))

# Normalize weights: each student appears 5 times, so divide by 5
# to maintain correct population total (Allison, 1999)
stacked5$weight_stacked <- stacked5$weight / 5

# Survey design on stacked data: cluster at PSU + student level
# because each student contributes 5 correlated rows
des_stacked5 <- svydesign(id = ~psu + student_id, strata = ~stratum,
                           weights = ~weight_stacked, data = stacked5, nest = TRUE)

# ─── FIT 5-SUBSTANCE STACKED MODEL ───
# Main effects + distress × outcome_type interactions
# NMPOU is reference: negative interaction = stronger for NMPOU than comparator
fit_stacked5 <- svyglm(
  Y ~ outcome_type + su_loo + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 +
      q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 +
      bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
      rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  design = des_stacked5, family = quasibinomial())

# Extract coefficient table for interaction terms
cc5 <- summary(fit_stacked5)$coefficients

# ─── JOINT WALD TEST: F2 distress interactions (bu_1 + sc_1; 8 terms) ───
# sv_1 moved to F3 in v32; tested individually below
wald_all5 <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")

# ─── JOINT WALD TEST: all variable interactions (24 terms) ───
wald_all5_full <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
    rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")

# ─── SEPARATE WALD TESTS per variable ───
wald_bu <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ bu_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_sc <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ sc_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_sv <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ sv_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_rb <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ rb_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_hh <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ hh_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_ms <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5, ~ ms_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")

# ─── 3-SUBSTANCE CONFIRMATORY MODEL ───
stacked3 <- bind_rows(r1, r2, r3)
stacked3$outcome_type <- factor(stacked3$outcome_type,
  levels = c("NMPOU", "Alcohol", "Marijuana"))

# Normalize weights for 3-substance model (3 rows per student)
stacked3$weight_stacked <- stacked3$weight / 3

des_stacked3 <- svydesign(id = ~psu + student_id, strata = ~stratum,
                           weights = ~weight_stacked, data = stacked3, nest = TRUE)

fit_stacked3 <- svyglm(
  Y ~ outcome_type + su_loo + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 +
      q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 +
      bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
      rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  design = des_stacked3, family = quasibinomial())

cc3 <- summary(fit_stacked3)$coefficients

wald_all3 <- regTermTest(fit_stacked3,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")
```

```{r khb-mediation, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 15: KHB MEDIATION DECOMPOSITION
# Purpose: Decompose each predictor's total NMPOU association into direct and indirect (polysubstance-mediated) components using the Karlson-Holm-Breen method.
# Notes: KHB corrects for the noncollapsibility of logistic regression, which inflates apparent mediation in naive comparisons. Results are interpreted as accounting decompositions, not causal mediation. Reference: Karlson, Holm & Breen (2012), Sociological Methodology, 42:286-313.
# ============================================================

#
# Manual KHB procedure:
#   Stage 1: Survey-weighted linear regress mediator (su_life) on distress + controls → residuals
#   Stage 2a: Logistic with su_life → coefficient = direct effect
#   Stage 2b: Logistic with residuals replacing su_life → coefficient = total effect
#   Indirect = total - direct (on log-odds scale)
#   % mediated = indirect / total * 100
#
# CAUTION: Cross-sectional data — results are ACCOUNTING DECOMPOSITIONS,
#          not causal mediation (Karlson, Holm and Breen, 2012).
###############################################################################
##
## METHODS NOTE (KHB — Karlson, Holm & Breen 2012; Breen, Karlson & Holm 2013):
##
## THE RESCALING PROBLEM IN LOGISTIC REGRESSION:
## In OLS, comparing coefficients between a "reduced" model (without mediator)
## and a "full" model (with mediator) cleanly decomposes total into direct +
## indirect effects. In logistic regression, this fails because the residual
## variance is fixed at pi^2/3 by the logistic distribution. Adding a variable
## does NOT absorb residual variance — instead the SCALE of all coefficients
## changes. A coefficient may shrink when a mediator is added even if there is
## zero mediation, simply because the model's implicit error variance shifted.
##
## THE KHB SOLUTION — RESIDUALIZATION:
## Instead of comparing reduced vs. full models (which differ in scale), KHB
## replaces the mediator Z with its residual from a regression of Z on X
## (the predictor of interest) and all other covariates W.
##
##   Stage 1: Z_resid = residuals( Z ~ X + W )        [linear/OLS]
##   Stage 2a: Y ~ X + Z + W                          [logistic — "full" model]
##   Stage 2b: Y ~ X + Z_resid + W                    [logistic — "total" model]
##
## KEY INSIGHT: Models 2a and 2b have the SAME number of predictors and
## therefore the SAME residual variance, so their coefficients are on the
## SAME SCALE. The difference in the coefficient on X between 2b and 2a
## is a pure confounding/mediation effect, free of rescaling artifacts.
##
## WHY STAGE 1 USES family = gaussian() (OLS):
## KHB requires the Stage 1 residuals to be orthogonal to X by construction.
## OLS guarantees this (a property of linear projection). For a binary
## mediator like su_life (0/1), Breen et al. (2013, p. 156) show that OLS
## residuals work correctly even though the mediator is binary — the key
## is orthogonality, not distributional correctness. Using logistic regression
## for Stage 1 would NOT guarantee orthogonality and would break the method.
##
## WHAT THE RESIDUALS REPRESENT:
## resid_su_bu = the part of su_life NOT linearly predicted by bu_1 (and W).
## In other words, it captures "polysubstance involvement explained by factors
## OTHER than bullying." When this residual replaces su_life in Stage 2b,
## the bu_1 coefficient captures the TOTAL effect of bullying on NMPOU
## (both the direct path and the path through polysubstance), because the
## residual is uncorrelated with bu_1 and thus does not "soak up" any of
## bullying's effect.
##
## INTERPRETING THE DECOMPOSITION:
##   beta_total  = coef on X from Model 2b (residualized) — total effect
##   beta_direct = coef on X from Model 2a (full model)   — direct effect
##   indirect    = beta_total - beta_direct                — mediated portion
##   % mediated  = (indirect / beta_total) * 100
##
## Because both betas are on the same scale (same number of predictors),
## the ratio (% mediated) is scale-free: sigma cancels in the numerator
## and denominator (Breen et al. 2013, eq. 7). This is why KHB % mediated
## is valid even without estimating Allison's delta.
##
## WHY DIRECT EFFECTS ARE SHARED ACROSS PREDICTORS:
## Below, beta_direct_sc and beta_direct_sv are extracted from fit_direct_bu
## (the same full model). This is correct: the "full model" (2a) is identical
## regardless of which predictor we decompose — it always includes all
## predictors + the mediator. Only the Stage 1 and Stage 2b models change
## (different residuals for each predictor).
##
## ACCOUNTING vs. CAUSAL DECOMPOSITION:
## With cross-sectional data, we cannot claim that su_life causally mediates
## the bu_1 → NMPOU path. The decomposition tells us what FRACTION of the
## bu_1 log-odds is "accounted for" by shared linear association with su_life.
## Causal mediation would require temporal ordering and no unmeasured
## confounders of the mediator-outcome relationship (sequential ignorability).
##

# Numeric version of mediator for survey-weighted linear stage
yrbs$su_life_num <- as.numeric(yrbs$su_life == "Yes")

# Complete cases for KHB (needs all M3 variables + mediator)
khb_vars <- c("q49_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1",
              "su_life", "su_life_num", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1",
              "psu", "stratum", "weight")
khb_data <- yrbs[complete.cases(yrbs[, khb_vars]), ]
n_khb <- nrow(khb_data)

# ─── KHB for bu_1 (bullying → su_life → NMPOU) ───
# Stage 1: Survey-weighted OLS of mediator on bu_1 + all other controls
des_khb <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                     data = khb_data, nest = TRUE)
stage1_bu <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ bu_1 + rb_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb, family = gaussian())
khb_data$resid_su_bu <- residuals(stage1_bu)
# Rebuild design after adding residuals
des_khb <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                     data = khb_data, nest = TRUE)

# Direct effect: coefficient on bu_1 in full model (with su_life)
fit_direct_bu <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ bu_1 + su_life + rb_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb, family = quasibinomial())
beta_direct_bu <- coef(fit_direct_bu)["bu_1Yes"]

# Total effect: coefficient on bu_1 when su_life replaced by residuals
fit_total_bu <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ bu_1 + resid_su_bu + rb_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_bu <- coef(fit_total_bu)["bu_1Yes"]

# Decomposition
indirect_bu <- beta_total_bu - beta_direct_bu
prop_med_bu <- (indirect_bu / beta_total_bu) * 100

# ─── KHB for sc_1 (suicidality → su_life → NMPOU) ───
stage1_sc <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ sc_1 + rb_1 + bu_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb, family = gaussian())
khb_data$resid_su_sc <- residuals(stage1_sc)
des_khb2 <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                      data = khb_data, nest = TRUE)

# Direct effect: sc_1 coefficient from same full model as bu_1
beta_direct_sc <- coef(fit_direct_bu)["sc_1Yes"]

# Total effect: sc_1 with residuals replacing su_life
fit_total_sc <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ sc_1 + resid_su_sc + rb_1 + bu_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb2, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_sc <- coef(fit_total_sc)["sc_1Yes"]

indirect_sc <- beta_total_sc - beta_direct_sc
prop_med_sc <- (indirect_sc / beta_total_sc) * 100

# ─── KHB for sv_1 (sexual victimization → su_life → NMPOU) ───
stage1_sv <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ sv_1 + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb, family = gaussian())
khb_data$resid_su_sv <- residuals(stage1_sv)
des_khb3 <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                      data = khb_data, nest = TRUE)

beta_direct_sv <- coef(fit_direct_bu)["sv_1Yes"]

fit_total_sv <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ sv_1 + resid_su_sv + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb3, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_sv <- coef(fit_total_sv)["sv_1Yes"]

indirect_sv <- beta_total_sv - beta_direct_sv
prop_med_sv <- (indirect_sv / beta_total_sv) * 100

# ─── KHB for hh_1 (household adversity → su_life → NMPOU) ───
# Requires subsample (national-module items)
khb_sub_vars <- c("q49_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1",
                   "su_life", "su_life_num", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1",
                   "hh_1", "ms_1", "psu", "stratum", "weight")
khb_sub_data <- yrbs[yrbs$in_subsample & complete.cases(yrbs[, khb_sub_vars]), ]
n_khb_sub <- nrow(khb_sub_data)

des_khb_sub <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                         data = khb_sub_data, nest = TRUE)
stage1_hh <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ hh_1 + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + ms_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb_sub, family = gaussian())
khb_sub_data$resid_su_hh <- residuals(stage1_hh)
des_khb_sub <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                         data = khb_sub_data, nest = TRUE)

fit_direct_hh <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ hh_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + ms_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb_sub, family = quasibinomial())
beta_direct_hh <- coef(fit_direct_hh)["hh_1Yes"]

fit_total_hh <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ hh_1 + resid_su_hh + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + ms_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb_sub, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_hh <- coef(fit_total_hh)["hh_1Yes"]

indirect_hh <- beta_total_hh - beta_direct_hh
prop_med_hh <- (indirect_hh / beta_total_hh) * 100

# ─── KHB for ms_1 (community safety → su_life → NMPOU) ───
stage1_ms <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ ms_1 + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb_sub, family = gaussian())
khb_sub_data$resid_su_ms <- residuals(stage1_ms)
des_khb_sub2 <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                          data = khb_sub_data, nest = TRUE)

beta_direct_ms <- coef(fit_direct_hh)["ms_1Yes"]

fit_total_ms <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ ms_1 + resid_su_ms + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb_sub2, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_ms <- coef(fit_total_ms)["ms_1Yes"]

indirect_ms <- beta_total_ms - beta_direct_ms
prop_med_ms <- (indirect_ms / beta_total_ms) * 100

# ─── KHB for rb_1 (risk behavior → su_life → NMPOU) ───
stage1_rb <- svyglm(su_life_num ~ rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
                     design = des_khb, family = gaussian())
khb_data$resid_su_rb <- residuals(stage1_rb)
des_khb4 <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                      data = khb_data, nest = TRUE)

beta_direct_rb <- coef(fit_direct_bu)["rb_1Yes"]

fit_total_rb <- svyglm(
  q49_1 ~ rb_1 + resid_su_rb + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1,
  design = des_khb4, family = quasibinomial())
beta_total_rb <- coef(fit_total_rb)["rb_1Yes"]

indirect_rb <- beta_total_rb - beta_direct_rb
prop_med_rb <- (indirect_rb / beta_total_rb) * 100
```

```{r sensitivity, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 16: SENSITIVITY ANALYSES (SM1-SM11)
# Purpose: Eleven pre-specified sensitivity analyses testing alternative operationalizations of composites, model specifications, and outcome definitions.
# Notes: SM1-SM11 test robustness of primary findings. SM1: persistent sadness replaces suicidality. SM2: dating violence added to sv_1. SM3: forced intercourse added to sc_1. SM4: expanded household (hh_2). SM5: early debut age groups. SM6: past-30-day polysubstance. SM7: 3-item risk behavior. SM8: restricted to national-module respondents. SM9: past-30-day NMPOU. SM10: polysubstance including cocaine. SM11: weapon carrying and physical fighting as separate predictors.
# ============================================================

#
# NOTE: S1-S7 use the original M5/M3 spec (without sv_1) for internal
#       consistency with the sensitivity design. The original M5 (without sv_1)
#       is also stored for S3 attenuation comparison.
###############################################################################
##
## METHODS NOTE (Sensitivity & Attenuation):
##
## ATTENUATION INTERPRETATION: compute_attenuation() compares a predictor's
## log-odds in two nested models (e.g., M5-orig vs. S3). The percentage
## change is reported as "attenuation." HOWEVER, this is a NAIVE measure
## that conflates two distinct phenomena (see METHODS NOTE in helper-functions):
##   (a) Genuine confounding — part of the predictor's effect is explained
##       by the newly added variable.
##   (b) Rescaling artifact — adding a variable changes the implicit residual
##       SD in logistic regression, shrinking ALL coefficients even without
##       confounding (Karlson, Holm & Breen 2012).
## The KHB method (CHUNK 15) separates these. The naive attenuation here
## is used for sensitivity reporting (Table S13), not for causal decomposition.
##
## S1-S5 LOGIC: Each sensitivity model swaps or adds a single variable to
## the M5 specification. If the core distress predictors (bu_1, sc_1) remain
## significant and of similar magnitude, the findings are robust to the
## specific operationalization of that construct.
##
## Q92 SENSITIVITY: Past-30-day NMPOU (Q92) is a stricter outcome than
## lifetime (Q49). Replicating key associations with Q92 supports that
## the findings are not driven by lifetime prevalence inflation.
##

# ─── Original M5 without sv_1 (needed for S3 attenuation baseline) ───
res_M5_orig <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "M5-orig")

# S1: Persistent sadness (q26_1) replaces suicidality (sc_1)
res_S1 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + q26_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "S1")

# S2: Dating violence added — daters subsample only
res_S2 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + dv_1 + sc_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_daters_sub, "S2")

# S3: Forced sexual intercourse (q19_1) added to M5 (without sv_1)
res_S3 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 + q19_1,
  des_sub, "S3")

# S3 attenuation: how much does adding q19_1 attenuate sc_1?
att_s3_sc1 <- compute_attenuation(res_M5_orig, res_S3, "sc_1Yes")

# S4: Expanded household adversity (hh_2 = hh_1 + Q102)
res_S4 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + hh_2 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "S4")

# S5: Early sexual debut (q57_5) added to M5 (without sv_1)
res_S5 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 + q57_5,
  des_sub, "S5")

# S6: Past-30-day polysubstance (su_1) replaces lifetime (su_life) — M3 spec
res_S6 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_1 + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1,
  des_full, "S6")

# S7: Three-item risk behavior (rb_1_3item = Q10 + Q12 + Q17) replaces rb_1 — M3 spec
res_S7 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1_3item + bu_1 + sc_1,
  des_full, "S7")

# ─── Q92 SENSITIVITY: Past-30-day NMPOU as outcome ───
# Q92 is national-only, so use subsample design
res_Q92_M3 <- fit_model(
  q92_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1,
  des_sub, "Q92-M3")

res_Q92_M5 <- fit_model(
  q92_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "Q92-M5")

# ─── SM10: Alternative polysubstance composite including cocaine ───
# ADDED IN v4 — SM10 four-substance composite
# Re-run M5 substituting su_life_coc (cigarettes + alcohol + marijuana + cocaine)
# for su_life (cigarettes + alcohol + marijuana). Reports bullying AOR.
res_SM10 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life_coc + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "SM10")

# ─── SM11: Weapon carrying and physical fighting as separate predictors ───
# ADDED IN v4 — SM11 separate weapon/fighting predictors
# Enters Q12 (weapon carrying) and Q17 (physical fighting) individually instead
# of the composite rb_1, testing measurement sensitivity of the risk behavior specification.
res_SM11 <- fit_model(
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + q12_1 + q17_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "SM11")

# NOT REPORTED IN MANUSCRIPT — RETAINED FOR REFERENCE
# MODIFIED IN v3 — The following two analyses (community safety count, ideation-only)
# were labeled SM8/SM9 in v2 but do not match the SM8/SM9 descriptions in the ground truth.
# MODIFIED IN v4 — Supplementary analysis C (risk items separate) promoted to SM11 above.

# ─── Supplementary analysis A: Community safety as count 0-3 ───
yrbs$ms_count <- as.numeric(yrbs$q14_1 == "Yes") +
                 as.numeric(yrbs$q15_1 == "Yes") +
                 as.numeric(yrbs$q18_1 == "Yes")
yrbs_sm8 <- yrbs
des_full_sm8 <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                           data = yrbs_sm8, nest = TRUE)
des_sub_sm8 <- subset(des_full_sm8, in_subsample == TRUE)
res_supp_A <- fit_model(  # MODIFIED IN v3 — renamed from res_SM8
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_count,
  des_sub_sm8, "Supp-A: ms_count")

# ─── Supplementary analysis B: Suicidality as ideation-only (Q27) ───
res_supp_B <- fit_model(  # MODIFIED IN v3 — renamed from res_SM9
  q49_1 ~ q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 + su_life + rb_1 + bu_1 + q27_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1,
  des_sub, "Supp-B: ideation-only")

# MODIFIED IN v4 — Supplementary analysis C (risk items separate) removed;
# promoted to SM11 (res_SM11) above.
```

```{r ame-specificity, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 17: AVERAGE MARGINAL EFFECTS — SUBSTANCE SPECIFICITY
# Purpose: Compute AMEs for each predictor across five substance outcomes (NMPOU, alcohol, marijuana, cigarettes, cocaine) to express associations on the probability scale.
# Notes: AMEs are computed via the marginaleffects package (Arel-Bundock et al., 2024). Unlike AORs, AMEs are directly comparable across models and substances because they are unaffected by noncollapsibility (Mood, 2010).
# ============================================================

#
# Reference: Allison PD. Comparing logit and probit coefficients across
#   groups. Sociol Methods Res. 1999;28(2):186-208.
# Implementation: Arel-Bundock V et al. JSS. 2024;111(9):1-32.
###############################################################################
##
## METHODS NOTE (Allison 1999 — Probability-Scale Fix):
## Logit coefficients estimate beta/sigma, where sigma is the residual SD.
## If sigma differs across substance types, coefficient comparisons are
## invalid. Predicted probabilities depend on the composite beta/sigma
## (which the model identifies), not on beta and sigma separately.
## Therefore AMEs (average differences in predicted P(Y=1)) are
## comparable across groups without estimating delta.
##

# ─── Extract fitted svyglm objects from drug-specific results ───
mod_nmpou <- attr(res_ds_nmpou, "model")
mod_alc   <- attr(res_ds_alc, "model")
mod_mar   <- attr(res_ds_mar, "model")
mod_cig   <- attr(res_ds_cig, "model")
mod_coc   <- attr(res_ds_coc, "model")

# Predictors to compare across substances (all 6 framework variables)
ame_vars <- c("rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "hh_1", "ms_1")

# ─── Compute AMEs for each substance ───
# avg_comparisons() with type = "response" gives the average change in
# P(Y=1) when the binary predictor switches from "No" to "Yes"
ame_nmpou <- avg_comparisons(mod_nmpou, variables = ame_vars, type = "response")
ame_alc   <- avg_comparisons(mod_alc,   variables = ame_vars, type = "response")
ame_mar   <- avg_comparisons(mod_mar,   variables = ame_vars, type = "response")
ame_cig   <- avg_comparisons(mod_cig,   variables = ame_vars, type = "response")
ame_coc   <- avg_comparisons(mod_coc,   variables = ame_vars, type = "response")

# ─── Polysubstance AMEs (leave-one-out names differ across models) ───
ame_su_nmpou <- avg_comparisons(mod_nmpou, variables = "su_life",    type = "response")
ame_su_alc   <- avg_comparisons(mod_alc,   variables = "su_loo_alc", type = "response")
ame_su_mar   <- avg_comparisons(mod_mar,   variables = "su_loo_mar", type = "response")
ame_su_cig   <- avg_comparisons(mod_cig,   variables = "su_loo_cig", type = "response")
ame_su_coc   <- avg_comparisons(mod_coc,   variables = "su_loo_coc", type = "response")

# Normalize polysubstance term names to "su_life" for consistent labeling
normalize_su <- function(df) { df$term <- "su_life"; df }

# ─── Combine into long table (7 predictors x 5 substances = 35 rows) ───
ame_all <- bind_rows(
  mutate(as.data.frame(ame_nmpou), substance = "NMPOU"),
  mutate(normalize_su(as.data.frame(ame_su_nmpou)), substance = "NMPOU"),
  mutate(as.data.frame(ame_alc),   substance = "Alcohol"),
  mutate(normalize_su(as.data.frame(ame_su_alc)),   substance = "Alcohol"),
  mutate(as.data.frame(ame_mar),   substance = "Marijuana"),
  mutate(normalize_su(as.data.frame(ame_su_mar)),   substance = "Marijuana"),
  mutate(as.data.frame(ame_cig),   substance = "Cigarettes"),
  mutate(normalize_su(as.data.frame(ame_su_cig)),   substance = "Cigarettes"),
  mutate(as.data.frame(ame_coc),   substance = "Cocaine"),
  mutate(normalize_su(as.data.frame(ame_su_coc)),   substance = "Cocaine")
)

# ─── Build Table S7 data frame ───
# Readable predictor labels (all 7 predictors including polysubstance)
ame_labels <- c(
  "su_life" = "Polysubstance use",
```
