```{r ame-specificity, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
  "bu_1"  = "Bullying victimization",
  "sc_1"  = "Suicidality",
  "sv_1"  = "Sexual victimization",
  "rb_1"  = "Risk behavior",
  "hh_1"  = "Household adversity",
  "ms_1"  = "Community safety adversity"
)
ame_all$predictor_label <- ame_labels[ame_all$term]

# Format for table: AME (95% CI)
ame_all$ame_fmt <- sprintf("%.3f (%.3f, %.3f)",
                           ame_all$estimate, ame_all$conf.low, ame_all$conf.high)
ame_all$p_fmt <- sapply(ame_all$p.value, function(p) {
  if (is.na(p)) return("--")
  if (p < 0.001) return("< .001")
  if (p < 0.01) return(sprintf("%.3f", p))
  sprintf("%.3f", p)
})

# Wide-format for Table S7
s11_wide <- ame_all %>%
  select(predictor_label, substance, ame_fmt, p_fmt) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = substance,
    values_from = c(ame_fmt, p_fmt),
    names_glue = "{substance}_{.value}"
  )

# ─── Save CSV ───
ame_csv <- ame_all %>%
  select(predictor = predictor_label, substance, estimate, std.error,
         conf.low, conf.high, p.value) %>%
  arrange(predictor, substance)
write.csv(ame_csv, here::here("tables", "Table_S5_AME_by_substance.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# ─── Verification anchor ───
stopifnot(
  nrow(ame_csv) == (length(ame_vars) + 1) * 5,  # 7 predictors x 5 substances = 35
  length(unique(ame_csv$substance)) == 5,        # all 5 substances
  all(c("Bullying victimization", "Suicidality", "Polysubstance use") %in%
      ame_csv$predictor)                         # key predictors present
)
cat("AME verification: ", nrow(ame_csv), "rows,",
    length(unique(ame_csv$substance)), "substances,",
    length(unique(ame_csv$predictor)), "predictors. OK.\n")

# ─── Store key AME values for inline R (all 7 predictors) ───
# Helper: extract AME stats for a given predictor and substance
extract_ame <- function(term_name, sub_name = "NMPOU") {
  row <- ame_all[ame_all$term == term_name & ame_all$substance == sub_name, ]
  list(
    est = round(row$estimate * 100, 1),
    lo  = round(row$conf.low * 100, 1),
    hi  = round(row$conf.high * 100, 1),
    p   = row$p.value
  )
}

# Helper: format AME inline as "X.X pp (95% CI: X.X to X.X)"
fmt_ame_inline <- function(term_name, sub_name = "NMPOU") {
  a <- extract_ame(term_name, sub_name)
  sprintf("%.1f pp, 95%% CI: %.1f to %.1f", a$est, a$lo, a$hi)
}

# Polysubstance use
ame_su_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("su_life", "NMPOU")
ame_su_nmpou_est <- ame_su_nmpou_stats$est
ame_su_nmpou_lo  <- ame_su_nmpou_stats$lo
ame_su_nmpou_hi  <- ame_su_nmpou_stats$hi

# Bullying
ame_bu_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("bu_1", "NMPOU")
ame_bu_nmpou_est <- ame_bu_nmpou_stats$est
ame_bu_nmpou_lo  <- ame_bu_nmpou_stats$lo
ame_bu_nmpou_hi  <- ame_bu_nmpou_stats$hi
ame_bu_nmpou_p   <- ame_bu_nmpou_stats$p

# Suicidality
ame_sc_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("sc_1", "NMPOU")
ame_sc_nmpou_est <- ame_sc_nmpou_stats$est
ame_sc_nmpou_lo  <- ame_sc_nmpou_stats$lo
ame_sc_nmpou_hi  <- ame_sc_nmpou_stats$hi

# Sexual victimization
ame_sv_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("sv_1", "NMPOU")
ame_sv_nmpou_est <- ame_sv_nmpou_stats$est

# Risk behavior
ame_rb_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("rb_1", "NMPOU")
ame_rb_nmpou_est <- ame_rb_nmpou_stats$est

# Household adversity
ame_hh_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("hh_1", "NMPOU")
ame_hh_nmpou_est <- ame_hh_nmpou_stats$est

# Community safety adversity
ame_ms_nmpou_stats <- extract_ame("ms_1", "NMPOU")
ame_ms_nmpou_est <- ame_ms_nmpou_stats$est

# Check: is bullying AME non-significant for all other substances?
ame_bu_other <- ame_all[ame_all$term == "bu_1" & ame_all$substance != "NMPOU", ]
ame_bu_other_all_ns <- all(ame_bu_other$p.value > 0.05)
# Range of other-substance bullying AMEs (percentage points)
ame_bu_other_range_lo <- round(min(ame_bu_other$estimate * 100), 1)
ame_bu_other_range_hi <- round(max(ame_bu_other$estimate * 100), 1)
```

```{r ame-primary-models, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 18: AVERAGE MARGINAL EFFECTS — PRIMARY MODELS
# Purpose: Compute AMEs for all predictors in the primary model sequence (M1-M5) to track how associations change as predictors are added.
# Notes: AMEs quantify the average change in predicted NMPOU probability (percentage points) when a predictor switches from absent to present, holding all other variables at their observed values.
# ============================================================

# M2a AME (F2 distress variables on full sample)
mod_M2a <- attr(res_M2a, "model")
ame_M2a <- as.data.frame(avg_comparisons(mod_M2a,
  variables = c("bu_1", "sc_1"), type = "response"))

# M2b AME (F3 adversity variables on subsample)
mod_M2b <- attr(res_M2b, "model")
ame_M2b <- as.data.frame(avg_comparisons(mod_M2b,
  variables = c("sv_1", "hh_1", "ms_1"), type = "response"))

# M3 AME (F1 + F2 on full sample)
mod_M3 <- attr(res_M3, "model")
ame_M3 <- as.data.frame(avg_comparisons(mod_M3,
  variables = c("su_life", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1"), type = "response"))

# M4 AME (+ sv_1 + hh_1 on subsample)
mod_M4 <- attr(res_M4, "model")
ame_M4 <- as.data.frame(avg_comparisons(mod_M4,
  variables = c("su_life", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "hh_1"),
  type = "response"))

# M5 AME (full ecological model on subsample) — same as ame_nmpou + su_life
mod_M5 <- attr(res_M5, "model")
ame_M5 <- as.data.frame(avg_comparisons(mod_M5,
  variables = c("su_life", "rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "hh_1", "ms_1"),
  type = "response"))

# Helper: format AME for table display (percentage points)
fmt_ame_cell <- function(ame_df, term_name) {
  row <- ame_df[ame_df$term == term_name, ]
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("")
  sprintf("%.1f (%.1f, %.1f)",
    row$estimate * 100, row$conf.low * 100, row$conf.high * 100)
}

cat("M1-M5 AMEs computed successfully.\n")
```

```{r ame-heterogeneity, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 19: AVERAGE MARGINAL EFFECTS — HETEROGENEITY TESTS
# Purpose: Test whether each predictor's AME differs significantly between NMPOU and each comparison substance using pairwise z-tests.
# Notes: Pairwise z-tests compare AME differences between substances. A significant z-test indicates that the predictor has a substance-specific association. This complements the joint Wald test from the stacked model.
# ============================================================

# ─── AMEs by substance type from stacked model ───
# The stacked model (fit_stacked5) has predictor:outcome_type interactions,
# so AMEs will naturally differ by outcome_type
# NOTE: sv_1 included for completeness even though it is now F3
distress_vars <- c("bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1")

ame_stacked_by_sub <- tryCatch({
  avg_comparisons(
    fit_stacked5,
    variables = distress_vars,
    by = "outcome_type",
    type = "response"
  )
}, error = function(e) {
  cat("AME stacked by substance FAILED:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
  NULL
})

# ─── Pairwise tests: each substance vs NMPOU reference ───
ame_het_results <- list()

if (!is.null(ame_stacked_by_sub)) {
  for (v in distress_vars) {
    het_test <- tryCatch({
      avg_comparisons(
        fit_stacked5,
        variables = v,
        by = "outcome_type",
        type = "response",
        hypothesis = "pairwise"
      )
    }, error = function(e) {
      cat("Pairwise AME test for", v, "FAILED:", conditionMessage(e), "\n")
      NULL
    })
    if (!is.null(het_test)) {
      ame_het_results[[v]] <- as.data.frame(het_test) %>%
        mutate(predictor = v, test_type = "pairwise", model_basis = "stacked")
    }
  }
}

# ─── Combine and save ───
if (length(ame_het_results) > 0) {
  ame_het_df <- bind_rows(ame_het_results)
  write.csv(ame_het_df,
            here::here("tables", "AME_heterogeneity_tests.csv"),
            row.names = FALSE)
  cat("AME heterogeneity tests saved:", nrow(ame_het_df), "rows.\n")

  # Extract joint p for bullying AME heterogeneity (for Results)
  ame_het_bu <- ame_het_df[ame_het_df$predictor == "bu_1", ]
  # The pairwise tests compare each pair; a significant result for NMPOU vs others
  # confirms heterogeneity. Store the minimum p across NMPOU-involving comparisons.
  ame_het_bu_min_p <- min(ame_het_bu$p.value, na.rm = TRUE)
} else {
  # ─── Fallback: manual z-tests from stacked AME table ───
  # Conservative (treats AMEs as independent), but transparent.
  # Each z-test compares the NMPOU AME to each other substance's AME.
  cat("NOTE: Pairwise hypothesis tests unavailable; using manual z-tests.\n")
  ame_df <- as.data.frame(ame_stacked_by_sub)
  het_rows <- list()
  for (v in distress_vars) {
    v_df <- ame_df[ame_df$term == v, ]
    nmpou_row <- v_df[v_df$outcome_type == "NMPOU", ]
    other_rows <- v_df[v_df$outcome_type != "NMPOU", ]
    if (nrow(nmpou_row) == 1 && nrow(other_rows) > 0) {
      diff_est <- other_rows$estimate - nmpou_row$estimate
      diff_se  <- sqrt(other_rows$std.error^2 + nmpou_row$std.error^2)
      z_stat   <- diff_est / diff_se
      p_val    <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z_stat))
      het_rows[[v]] <- data.frame(
        predictor = v,
        comparison = paste0(other_rows$outcome_type, " - NMPOU"),
        estimate = diff_est, std.error = diff_se,
        z.value = z_stat, p.value = p_val,
        test_type = "manual_z", model_basis = "stacked",
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    }
  }
  if (length(het_rows) > 0) {
    ame_het_df <- bind_rows(het_rows)
    write.csv(ame_het_df,
              here::here("tables", "AME_heterogeneity_tests.csv"),
              row.names = FALSE)
    cat("AME heterogeneity z-tests saved:", nrow(ame_het_df), "rows.\n")
    ame_het_bu <- ame_het_df[ame_het_df$predictor == "bu_1", ]
    ame_het_bu_min_p <- min(ame_het_bu$p.value, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else {
    cat("WARNING: AME heterogeneity tests could not be computed.\n")
    ame_het_bu_min_p <- NA
  }
}

# ─── Store stacked AME values for inline R ───
if (!is.null(ame_stacked_by_sub)) {
  ame_stacked_df <- as.data.frame(ame_stacked_by_sub)
  write.csv(ame_stacked_df,
            here::here("tables", "AME_stacked_by_substance.csv"),
            row.names = FALSE)
}
```

```{r stacked-lpm, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 20: STACKED LINEAR PROBABILITY MODEL
# Purpose: Replicate the stacked-outcome heterogeneity test using a linear probability model (LPM) as a robustness check against potential logistic model misspecification.
# Notes: LPM avoids the noncollapsibility issue inherent in logistic regression (Allison, 1999). Consistent Wald test results across logistic and LPM models strengthen specificity claims. Y must be numeric 0/1, not factor.
# ============================================================

# Convert Y to numeric for LPM (gaussian family requires 0/1, not factor)
stacked5$Y_num <- as.numeric(stacked5$Y == "Yes")
des_stacked5_lpm <- svydesign(id = ~psu + student_id, strata = ~stratum,
                               weights = ~weight_stacked, data = stacked5, nest = TRUE)

fit_stacked5_lpm <- svyglm(
  Y_num ~ outcome_type + su_loo + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 +
      q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 +
      bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
      rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  design = des_stacked5_lpm, family = gaussian())

# Joint Wald test: F2 distress interactions (bu_1 + sc_1 only; sv_1 now F3)
wald_lpm_distress <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_lpm,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")

# Joint Wald test: all variable interactions
wald_lpm_all <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_lpm,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
    rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")

# Per-variable Wald tests
wald_lpm_bu <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_lpm, ~ bu_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_lpm_sc <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_lpm, ~ sc_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")
wald_lpm_sv <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_lpm, ~ sv_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")

# Save results
lpm_results <- data.frame(
  test = c("Distress interactions (joint)",
           "All variable interactions (joint)",
           "Bullying x substance", "Suicidality x substance",
           "Sexual victimization x substance"),
  F_stat = round(c(wald_lpm_distress$Ftest, wald_lpm_all$Ftest,
                    wald_lpm_bu$Ftest, wald_lpm_sc$Ftest, wald_lpm_sv$Ftest), 2),
  df = c(paste(wald_lpm_distress$df, collapse = ","),
         paste(wald_lpm_all$df, collapse = ","),
         paste(wald_lpm_bu$df, collapse = ","),
         paste(wald_lpm_sc$df, collapse = ","),
         paste(wald_lpm_sv$df, collapse = ",")),
  p_value = c(wald_lpm_distress$p, wald_lpm_all$p,
              wald_lpm_bu$p, wald_lpm_sc$p, wald_lpm_sv$p),
  model = "LPM (gaussian)",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
write.csv(lpm_results, here::here("tables", "LPM_stacked_sensitivity.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)
cat("LPM sensitivity: distress interaction F =", round(wald_lpm_distress$Ftest, 2),
    ", p =", format.pval(wald_lpm_distress$p, digits = 4), "\n")
```

```{r stacked-relaxed, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 21: STACKED RELAXED-CONSTRAINT MODEL
# Purpose: Fit a stacked logistic model allowing all main effects to vary freely across substances (relaxed constraint) and test the joint significance of interaction terms.
# Notes: The relaxed model estimates substance-specific coefficients for all predictors. The interaction Wald test evaluates whether the full set of interactions improves model fit over the constrained model.
# ============================================================

fit_stacked5_relaxed <- svyglm(
  Y ~ outcome_type + su_loo + rb_1 + bu_1 + sc_1 + sv_1 + hh_1 + ms_1 +
      q2_1 + q3_1 + q4_1 +
      su_loo:outcome_type +                                # RELAXED
      bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type + sv_1:outcome_type +
      rb_1:outcome_type + hh_1:outcome_type + ms_1:outcome_type,
  design = des_stacked5, family = quasibinomial())

# Wald test on F2 distress interactions with relaxed constraint
wald_relaxed_distress <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_relaxed,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type + sc_1:outcome_type,
  method = "Wald")

wald_relaxed_bu <- regTermTest(fit_stacked5_relaxed,
  ~ bu_1:outcome_type, method = "Wald")

# Save results
relaxed_results <- data.frame(
  test = c("Distress interactions (joint, relaxed su_loo)",
           "Bullying x substance (relaxed su_loo)"),
  F_stat = round(c(wald_relaxed_distress$Ftest, wald_relaxed_bu$Ftest), 2),
  df = c(paste(wald_relaxed_distress$df, collapse = ","),
         paste(wald_relaxed_bu$df, collapse = ",")),
  p_value = c(wald_relaxed_distress$p, wald_relaxed_bu$p),
  model = "Logit (su_loo relaxed)",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
write.csv(relaxed_results,
          here::here("tables", "stacked_relaxed_constraint.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)
cat("Relaxed constraint: distress F =", round(wald_relaxed_distress$Ftest, 2),
    ", p =", format.pval(wald_relaxed_distress$p, digits = 4), "\n")
```

```{r khb-bootstrap, message=FALSE, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 22: KHB BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVALS
# Purpose: Compute bootstrap confidence intervals for KHB percent-mediated estimates using survey-weighted resampling.
# Notes: B=500 bootstrap iterations with survey weights. Jackknife CIs computed as sensitivity check. Results reported in Table S11. Reference: Karlson, Holm & Breen (2012).
# ============================================================

#
# Approach: Convert the survey design to replicate weights (JKn), then
#           for each replicate, re-run the full KHB procedure and collect
#           the % mediated. The jackknife variance formula then gives SEs.
###############################################################################

# ─── Helper: KHB decomposition for one predictor given data + weights ───
khb_decompose <- function(data, wts, predictor, other_predictors, outcome = "q49_1",
                          mediator = "su_life", mediator_num = "su_life_num") {
  # Stage 1: OLS of mediator on predictor + all other controls
  rhs_s1 <- paste(c(predictor, other_predictors), collapse = " + ")
  f_s1 <- as.formula(paste(mediator_num, "~", rhs_s1))
  s1 <- glm(f_s1, data = data, weights = wts, family = gaussian())
  data$resid_z <- residuals(s1)

  # Stage 2a: Direct effect (full model with mediator)
  rhs_direct <- paste(c(predictor, mediator, other_predictors), collapse = " + ")
  f_direct <- as.formula(paste(outcome, "~", rhs_direct))
  s2a <- glm(f_direct, data = data, weights = wts, family = quasibinomial())
  beta_direct <- coef(s2a)[paste0(predictor, "Yes")]

  # Stage 2b: Total effect (residuals replacing mediator)
  rhs_total <- paste(c(predictor, "resid_z", other_predictors), collapse = " + ")
  f_total <- as.formula(paste(outcome, "~", rhs_total))
  s2b <- glm(f_total, data = data, weights = wts, family = quasibinomial())
  beta_total <- coef(s2b)[paste0(predictor, "Yes")]

  # % mediated (scale-free)
  # unname() strips coefficient name (e.g., "bu_1Yes") so that c(est = ...)
  # in run_jk() does not create nested names like "est.bu_1Yes" → avoids NA
  if (abs(beta_total) < 1e-10) return(NA_real_)
  unname(((beta_total - beta_direct) / beta_total) * 100)
}

# ─── Define control variables for each predictor ───
controls_full <- c("rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1")
controls_sub  <- c("rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "ms_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1")
controls_sub_hh <- c("rb_1", "bu_1", "sc_1", "sv_1", "hh_1", "q2_1", "q3_1", "q4_1")

# ─── PSU-level jackknife for full-sample predictors ───
# Get unique PSUs and their strata
psu_strata <- unique(khb_data[, c("psu", "stratum")])
n_psu <- nrow(psu_strata)

# Function to run jackknife for one predictor
run_jk <- function(data, predictor, other_preds) {
  # Remove predictor from its own controls list
  ctrl <- setdiff(other_preds, predictor)

  # Full-sample estimate
  theta_full <- khb_decompose(data, data$weight, predictor, ctrl)

  # Jackknife: delete one PSU at a time
  psu_ids <- unique(data$psu)
  theta_jk <- vapply(psu_ids, function(p) {
    d_jk <- data[data$psu != p, ]
    tryCatch(
      khb_decompose(d_jk, d_jk$weight, predictor, ctrl),
      error = function(e) NA_real_
    )
  }, numeric(1))

  # JKn variance: (n-1)/n * sum((theta_jk - theta_full)^2)
  valid <- !is.na(theta_jk)
  n_valid <- sum(valid)
  if (n_valid < 2) return(c(est = theta_full, se = NA, ci_lo = NA, ci_hi = NA))
  jk_var <- ((n_valid - 1) / n_valid) * sum((theta_jk[valid] - theta_full)^2)
  jk_se <- sqrt(jk_var)

  c(est = theta_full, se = jk_se,
    ci_lo = theta_full - 1.96 * jk_se,
    ci_hi = theta_full + 1.96 * jk_se)
}

# Run for full-sample predictors
jk_bu <- run_jk(khb_data, "bu_1", controls_full)
jk_sc <- run_jk(khb_data, "sc_1", controls_full)
jk_sv <- run_jk(khb_data, "sv_1", controls_full)
jk_rb <- run_jk(khb_data, "rb_1", controls_full)

# Run for subsample predictors (hh_1, ms_1)
jk_hh <- run_jk(khb_sub_data, "hh_1", c(controls_sub, "ms_1"))
jk_ms <- run_jk(khb_sub_data, "ms_1", c(controls_sub_hh, "hh_1"))

# ─── Assemble KHB CI results ───
khb_ci_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("Bullying victimization", "Suicidality", "Sexual victimization",
               "Risk behavior", "Household adversity", "Community safety adversity"),
  Pct_Mediated = round(c(jk_bu["est"], jk_sc["est"], jk_sv["est"],
                          jk_rb["est"], jk_hh["est"], jk_ms["est"]), 1),
  SE = round(c(jk_bu["se"], jk_sc["se"], jk_sv["se"],
               jk_rb["se"], jk_hh["se"], jk_ms["se"]), 1),
  CI_lo = round(c(jk_bu["ci_lo"], jk_sc["ci_lo"], jk_sv["ci_lo"],
                   jk_rb["ci_lo"], jk_hh["ci_lo"], jk_ms["ci_lo"]), 1),
  CI_hi = round(c(jk_bu["ci_hi"], jk_sc["ci_hi"], jk_sv["ci_hi"],
                   jk_rb["ci_hi"], jk_hh["ci_hi"], jk_ms["ci_hi"]), 1),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
write.csv(khb_ci_df, here::here("tables", "KHB_mediation_CIs.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)
cat("KHB jackknife CIs computed for", nrow(khb_ci_df), "predictors.\n")

# Store for inline R
khb_bu_ci <- paste0(jk_bu["ci_lo"] %>% round(1), "-", jk_bu["ci_hi"] %>% round(1))
khb_sc_ci <- paste0(jk_sc["ci_lo"] %>% round(1), "-", jk_sc["ci_hi"] %>% round(1))
```

```{r table1-prevalence, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 23: TABLE 1 — PREVALENCE
# Purpose: Compute survey-weighted NMPOU prevalence by demographic and composite predictor categories for Table 1 (main text).
# Notes: Uses svymean() for prevalence and svychisq() for bivariate tests. Output formatted via ft_out() for Word document.
# ============================================================

# TABLE 1: Survey-Weighted Prevalence of Lifetime NMPOU
# Purpose: Display NMPOU prevalence by demographics and composite status.
# Columns: Characteristic, unweighted N, weighted %, NMPOU prevalence, 95% CI.
# Proper labels are used throughout (no variable names).
###############################################################################

# Helper: compute weighted NMPOU prevalence with 95% CI for a subgroup level
prev_row <- function(des, var_name, level, label) {
  # Subset the survey design to this level of the variable
  sub <- subset(des, eval(parse(text = paste0(var_name, " == '", level, "'"))))
  # Unweighted sample size for this level
  n <- nrow(sub)
  # Weighted NMPOU prevalence within the subgroup
  p <- svymean(~q49_1, sub, na.rm = TRUE)
  # Extract estimate and standard error, convert to percentage
  est <- 100 * coef(p)["q49_1Yes"]
  se  <- 100 * SE(p)["q49_1Yes"]
  # Wald-type 95% CI
  lo <- est - 1.96 * se
  hi <- est + 1.96 * se
  # Return formatted row
  data.frame(
    Characteristic = label,
    N = format(n, big.mark = ","),
    Prevalence = sprintf("%.1f", est),
    CI = paste0(sprintf("%.1f", lo), "-", sprintf("%.1f", hi)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

# Helper: compute missingness percentage for a variable
get_missing_pct <- function(var_name, data = yrbs, denom = nrow(yrbs)) {
  n_miss <- sum(is.na(data[[var_name]]))
  sprintf("%.1f", 100 * n_miss / denom)
}

# Helper: compute weighted percentage of a factor level in the full design
get_wt_pct <- function(des, var_name, level) {
  # Use svymean on factor variable to get proportions for each level
  frml <- as.formula(paste0("~", var_name))
  dist <- svymean(frml, des, na.rm = TRUE)
  # Extract the proportion for the specified level
  coef_name <- paste0(var_name, level)
  pct <- 100 * coef(dist)[coef_name]
  sprintf("%.1f", pct)
}

# ─── OVERALL ───
overall_p <- svymean(~q49_1, des_full, na.rm = TRUE)
overall_est <- 100 * coef(overall_p)["q49_1Yes"]
overall_se  <- 100 * SE(overall_p)["q49_1Yes"]
overall_lo  <- overall_est - 1.96 * overall_se
overall_hi  <- overall_est + 1.96 * overall_se

rows <- list()
rows[[1]] <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = "Overall",
  N = format(sum(!is.na(yrbs$q49_1)), big.mark = ","),
  Wt_Pct = "",
  Missing = get_missing_pct("q49_1"),
  Prevalence = sprintf("%.1f", overall_est),
  CI = paste0(sprintf("%.1f", overall_lo), "-", sprintf("%.1f", overall_hi)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# ─── SEX ───
rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = "Sex", N = "", Wt_Pct = "", Missing = get_missing_pct("q2_1"),
  Prevalence = "", CI = "", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
for (lev in c("Female", "Male")) {
  r <- prev_row(des_full, "q2_1", lev, paste0("  ", lev))
  r$Wt_Pct <- get_wt_pct(des_full, "q2_1", lev)
  r$Missing <- ""
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- r
}

# ─── RACE/ETHNICITY ───
rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = "Race/Ethnicity", N = "", Wt_Pct = "", Missing = get_missing_pct("q4_1"),
  Prevalence = "", CI = "", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
for (lev in c("White", "Black", "Latine/Multiple Latine",
              "Multiple Non-Latine", "Other")) {
  r <- prev_row(des_full, "q4_1", lev, paste0("  ", lev))
  r$Wt_Pct <- get_wt_pct(des_full, "q4_1", lev)
  r$Missing <- ""
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- r
}

# ─── GRADE ───
rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = "Grade", N = "", Wt_Pct = "", Missing = get_missing_pct("q3_1"),
  Prevalence = "", CI = "", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
for (lev in c("9th", "10th", "11th", "12th")) {
  r <- prev_row(des_full, "q3_1", lev, paste0("  ", lev))
  r$Wt_Pct <- get_wt_pct(des_full, "q3_1", lev)
  r$Missing <- ""
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- r
}

# ─── COMPOSITE PREDICTORS (binary: No/Yes) ───
# Use full sample for standard-version composites; subsample for national-only
comp_info <- list(
  list(var = "su_life", label = "Lifetime polysubstance use",     des = des_full),
  list(var = "rb_1",    label = "Risk behavior",                  des = des_full),
  list(var = "bu_1",    label = "Bullying victimization",         des = des_full),
  list(var = "sc_1",    label = "Suicidality",                    des = des_full),
  list(var = "sv_1",    label = "Sexual victimization",           des = des_full),
  list(var = "hh_1",    label = "Household adversity",             des = des_sub, sup = TRUE),
  list(var = "ms_1",    label = "Community safety adversity",      des = des_sub, sup = TRUE)
)

for (ci in comp_info) {
  # Compute missingness: use subsample denominator for national-only items
  denom <- if (isTRUE(ci$sup)) n_sub else nrow(yrbs)
  miss_data <- if (isTRUE(ci$sup)) yrbs[yrbs$in_subsample, ] else yrbs
  miss_pct <- get_missing_pct(ci$var, data = miss_data, denom = denom)
  # Header row for this composite
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.frame(
    Characteristic = ci$label,
    N = "", Wt_Pct = "", Missing = miss_pct, Prevalence = "", CI = "",
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  # No and Yes rows with weighted %
  for (lev in c("No", "Yes")) {
    r <- prev_row(ci$des, ci$var, lev, paste0("  ", lev))
    r$Wt_Pct <- get_wt_pct(ci$des, ci$var, lev)
    r$Missing <- ""
    rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- r
  }
}

# Combine all rows into one data frame
t1_df <- bind_rows(rows)
names(t1_df) <- c("Characteristic", "n", "Weighted %", "Missing (%)",
                   "NMPOU Prevalence (%)", "95% CI")

# Identify group-header rows (those with empty N column) for programmatic bold
group_rows <- which(t1_df[[2]] == "")

# Identify rows needing superscript "a" (Household adversity, Community safety)
hh_row <- which(t1_df$Characteristic == "Household adversity")
ms_row <- which(t1_df$Characteristic == "Community safety adversity")

# Output formatted table with bold group headers and footer notes
t1_notes <- paste0(
  "Unweighted Ns and survey-weighted percentages are reported per CDC guidance. ",
  "YRBS weights are calibrated for proportion estimation rather than population enumeration. ",
  "95% CI = Wald-type confidence interval. ",
  "(a) Household adversity and community safety adversity were administered only on the national ",
  "questionnaire module (subsample n = ", format(n_sub, big.mark = ","),
  "); all other variables were computed on the full sample (N = ", format(n_full, big.mark = ","), "). ",
  "Lifetime polysubstance use = any cigarette, alcohol, or marijuana use (Q31, Q41, Q46). ",
  "Risk behavior = weapon carrying or physical fighting (Q12, Q17). ",
  "Bullying victimization = school or electronic bullying (Q24, Q25). ",
  "Suicidality = seriously considered, planned, or attempted suicide (Q27, Q28, Q29). ",
  "Sexual victimization = forced sexual intercourse or sexual debut at age 12 or younger (Q19, Q57). ",
  "Household adversity = parental intimate partner violence, household substance use, or household mental illness (Q91, Q100, Q101). ",
  "Community safety adversity = unsafe school absences, weapon threats at school, or unsafe neighborhood (Q14, Q15, Q18)."
)

ft1 <- ft_out(t1_df,
  cap = "Table 1. Survey-Weighted Prevalence of Lifetime NMPOU by Demographic Characteristics and Composite Predictor Status",
  align_spec = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  notes = t1_notes)
ft1 <- bold(ft1, i = group_rows, j = 1)

# Add superscript "a" to Household adversity and Community safety rows
ft1 <- compose(ft1, i = hh_row, j = 1, value = as_paragraph(
  "Household adversity", as_sup("a")))
ft1 <- compose(ft1, i = ms_row, j = 1, value = as_paragraph(
  "Community safety adversity", as_sup("a")))
ft1
save_as_docx(ft1, path = here::here("figures", "Table1_Prevalence.docx"))
```

```{r figure1-forest, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=7, fig.height=5}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 24: FIGURE 1 — FOREST PLOT (AME)
# Purpose: Create a horizontal forest plot showing average marginal effects (percentage points) for all seven composite predictors from the primary model (M5).
# Notes: AME labels positioned above CI lines. Times New Roman font. Saved as 300 DPI TIFF for journal submission.
# ============================================================

# FIGURE 1: Forest Plot of M5 Average Marginal Effects (AME)
# Displays all 7 primary composite AMEs on the probability scale from the
# full ecological model (M5). AMEs give the average change in P(Y=1) when
# a binary predictor switches from "No" to "Yes", expressed in percentage
# points. AMEs are collapsible and directly comparable across models.
###############################################################################

# Named color palette used consistently across Figures 1 and 2
var_colors <- c(
  "Polysubstance use" = "#1b9e77", "Risk behavior" = "#d95f02",
  "Bullying" = "#7570b3", "Suicidality" = "#e7298a",
  "Sexual victimization" = "#66a61e", "Household adversity" = "#e6ab02",
  "Community safety" = "#a6761d"
)

# Build data frame from M5 AMEs (ame_M5 computed in ame-primary-models chunk)
fig1_ame_terms <- c("su_life", "sc_1", "rb_1", "ms_1", "bu_1", "sv_1", "hh_1")
fig1_ame_labels <- c("Polysubstance use", "Suicidality", "Risk behavior",
                     "Community safety", "Bullying",
                     "Sexual victimization", "Household adversity")

fig1_df <- ame_M5[ame_M5$term %in% fig1_ame_terms, ]
fig1_df$label <- fig1_ame_labels[match(fig1_df$term, fig1_ame_terms)]
# Convert to percentage points for plotting
fig1_df$AME_pp  <- fig1_df$estimate * 100
fig1_df$CI_lo   <- fig1_df$conf.low * 100
fig1_df$CI_hi   <- fig1_df$conf.high * 100
fig1_df$signif  <- ifelse(fig1_df$p.value < 0.05, "p < .05", "n.s.")
fig1_df <- fig1_df[order(fig1_df$AME_pp), ]
fig1_df$label <- factor(fig1_df$label, levels = fig1_df$label)

# Create AME forest plot
fig1_plot <- ggplot(fig1_df, aes(x = AME_pp, y = label, color = label, shape = signif)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = CI_lo, xmax = CI_hi), size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", AME_pp)), nudge_y = 0.3, size = 3.2, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = var_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("p < .05" = 16, "n.s." = 1)) +
  labs(x = "Average Marginal Effect (percentage points)", y = NULL,
       shape = "Significance",
       caption = paste(strwrap(paste0(
                        "n = ", format(attr(res_M5, "n"), big.mark = ","),
                        ". AME = average change in predicted probability of nonmedical ",
                        "prescription opioid use when predictor switches from absent to present. ",
                        "All models adjust for sex, grade, and race/ethnicity."), width = 80),
                        collapse = "\n")) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(text = element_text(family = "sans"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0, lineheight = 1.1))
print(fig1_plot)
ggsave(here::here("figures", "Figure1_ForestPlot_M5.tiff"), fig1_plot,
       width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300, compression = "lzw")
```

```{r figure2-specificity, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=7, fig.height=7}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 25: FIGURE 2 — SUBSTANCE SPECIFICITY PLOT
# Purpose: Create a dot plot comparing AMEs for each predictor across five substance outcomes (NMPOU, alcohol, marijuana, cigarettes, cocaine).
# Notes: Labels above upper CI bound to prevent overlap. Each substance model uses LOO polysubstance composites. Saved as 300 DPI TIFF.
# ============================================================

# FIGURE 2: Substance-Specific Average Marginal Effects With 95% CIs
#
# Design for Pediatrics (AAP):
#   - Sans-serif font (Arial) per AAP figure guidelines
#   - Abbreviations redefined in figure footnote
#   - Linear y-axis in percentage points (directly interpretable)
#   - Framework-consistent colors (F1 orange, F2 blue, F3 green)
#     matching Figure 3's Euler diagrams
#   - Width 7 inches (Pediatrics max), height 7 inches (within 9.25 max)
###############################################################################

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# ─── Build data frame from ame_all ───
fig2_df <- ame_all[, c("term", "estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high", "p.value",
                        "substance", "predictor_label")]
fig2_df$AME_pp <- fig2_df$estimate * 100
fig2_df$CI_lo  <- fig2_df$conf.low * 100
fig2_df$CI_hi  <- fig2_df$conf.high * 100

# ─── Variable labels and ordering ───
fig2_label_map <- c(
  "su_life" = "Polysubstance use", "rb_1" = "Risk behavior",
  "bu_1" = "Bullying", "sc_1" = "Suicidality",
  "sv_1" = "Sexual victimization", "hh_1" = "Household adversity",
  "ms_1" = "Community safety"
)
fig2_df$Variable <- fig2_label_map[fig2_df$term]

substance_names <- c("NMPOU", "Alcohol", "Marijuana", "Cigarettes", "Cocaine")
fig2_df$Substance <- factor(fig2_df$substance, levels = substance_names)

fig2_df$Variable <- factor(fig2_df$Variable,
  levels = c("Polysubstance use", "Risk behavior",
             "Bullying", "Suicidality",
             "Sexual victimization", "Household adversity",
             "Community safety"))

fig2_df$signif <- ifelse(fig2_df$p.value < 0.05, "p < .05", "n.s.")

# ─── Framework assignment ───
fig2_df$Framework <- case_when(
  fig2_df$term %in% c("su_life", "rb_1")       ~ "F1: Risk-Taking",
  fig2_df$term %in% c("bu_1", "sc_1")          ~ "F2: Distress",
  fig2_df$term %in% c("sv_1", "hh_1", "ms_1") ~ "F3: Adversity"
)

# ─── Color palette: framework-consistent, distinguishing variables ───
var_colors_fw <- c(
  "Polysubstance use"    = "#D55E00",  # F1 dark orange
  "Risk behavior"        = "#E8A060",  # F1 light orange
  "Bullying"             = "#0072B2",  # F2 dark blue
  "Suicidality"          = "#56B4E9",  # F2 light blue
  "Sexual victimization" = "#009E73",  # F3 dark green
  "Household adversity"  = "#5AC8A0",  # F3 medium green
  "Community safety"     = "#A0D8C8"   # F3 light green
)

# ─── Figure note (Pediatrics: abbreviations redefined in footnote) ───
fig_note <- paste(strwrap(paste0(
  "Note: Each substance model uses the fully adjusted specification (M5) ",
  "with a leave-one-out polysubstance composite. Filled circles indicate ",
  "p < .05; open circles indicate nonsignificant associations (p \u2265 .05). ",
  "Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals. ",
  "AME = average marginal effect; ",
  "NMPOU = nonmedical prescription opioid use."), width = 80), collapse = "\n")

# ─── Plot ───
fig2_plot <- ggplot(fig2_df,
    aes(x = Substance, y = AME_pp, color = Variable, shape = signif,
        group = Variable)) +
  # Zero reference line
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50",
             linewidth = 0.4) +
  # Error bars (behind points)
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = CI_lo, ymax = CI_hi),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
                width = 0.15, linewidth = 0.45) +
  # Points
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), size = 2) +
  # AME value labels (significant effects only, above upper CI)
  geom_text(data = subset(fig2_df, p.value < 0.05),
            aes(y = CI_hi, label = sprintf("%.1f", AME_pp)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 2.2, fontface = "bold",
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  # Scales
  scale_color_manual(
    values = var_colors_fw,
    guide = guide_legend(order = 1, nrow = 2, byrow = FALSE,
                         override.aes = list(size = 2.5))) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c("p < .05" = 16, "n.s." = 1),
    guide = guide_legend(order = 2,
                         override.aes = list(size = 2.5, color = "gray30"))) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.10))) +
  # Labels
  labs(
    y = "Average Marginal Effect (percentage points)",
    x = NULL,
    color = "Variable",
    shape = "Significance",
    caption = fig_note) +
  # Theme: sans-serif per Pediatrics guidelines
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    legend.spacing.y = unit(2, "pt"),
    legend.margin = margin(t = 4, b = 0),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    legend.key.size = unit(12, "pt"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 10, margin = margin(r = 6)),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 7.5, hjust = 0, color = "gray20",
                                 margin = margin(t = 8)),
    plot.margin = margin(t = 6, r = 8, b = 4, l = 4))

print(fig2_plot)

ggsave(here::here("figures", "Figure2_SubstanceSpecificity.tiff"), fig2_plot,
       width = 7, height = 7, dpi = 300, compression = "lzw")
```

```{r figure3-framework, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=7, fig.height=9}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 26: FIGURE 3 — FRAMEWORK ENDORSEMENT EULER DIAGRAMS
# Purpose: Create three-panel proportional Euler diagrams showing framework endorsement among NMPOU-positive adolescents by race/ethnicity.
# Notes: Panel A: F1 only. Panel B: F1 vs F2 (bullying + suicidality). Panel C: F1 vs F2 vs F3 (full three-framework). Circle sizes are proportional using Panel C euler() fit. eulerr v7.0.4.
# ============================================================

# FIGURE 3: Three-Panel Framework Endorsement Euler Diagrams by Race/Ethnicity
# Panel A (top):    F1 only             — national-module subsample
# Panel B (middle): F1 vs. F2           — national-module subsample
# Panel C (bottom): F1 vs. F2 vs. F3   — national-module subsample
#
# Colors: F1 = orange, F2 = sky blue, F3 = bluish green (colorblind-safe)
#
# KEY DESIGN DECISIONS:
#   - shape = "circle" in ALL euler() calls (no ellipses)
#   - Panels A/B: grid graphics with exact geometry from Panel C
#   - ALL five diagram columns have EQUAL width so circles are same size
#   - clip = "off" on viewports so labels are never truncated
#   - Tight top padding to minimize blank space above circles
#   - make_euler_3fw() defined here; reused by Figure S1
###############################################################################

library(eulerr)
library(patchwork)
library(grid)
library(survey)

# ─── Color palette (colorblind-safe) ───
col_f1 <- "#D55E00"  # Orange       (F1: Generalized Risk-Taking)
col_f2 <- "#56B4E9"  # Sky blue     (F2: Distress Relief)
col_f3 <- "#009E73"  # Bluish green (F3: Contextual Adversity)

# Ethnicity groups and display labels
eth_groups <- c("Latine/Multiple Latine", "White", "Black",
                "Other", "Multiple Non-Latine")
eth_labels <- c("Latine", "White", "Black", "Other", "Multiple\nNon-Latine")

# ─── Full sample data ───
nmpou_pos_full <- yrbs[!is.na(yrbs$q49_1) & yrbs$q49_1 == "Yes", ]
n_panel_a <- nrow(nmpou_pos_full)
nmpou_pos_full$F1 <- (nmpou_pos_full$su_life == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_full$rb_1 == "Yes")
nmpou_pos_full$F2 <- (nmpou_pos_full$bu_1 == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_full$sc_1 == "Yes")
nmpou_pos_full$F1[is.na(nmpou_pos_full$F1)] <- FALSE
nmpou_pos_full$F2[is.na(nmpou_pos_full$F2)] <- FALSE
des_panelA <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                         data = nmpou_pos_full, nest = TRUE)

# ─── National-module subsample data: 3 frameworks ───
nmpou_pos_sub <- yrbs[yrbs$in_subsample & !is.na(yrbs$q49_1) & yrbs$q49_1 == "Yes", ]
n_panel_b <- nrow(nmpou_pos_sub)
nmpou_pos_sub$F1 <- (nmpou_pos_sub$su_life == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_sub$rb_1 == "Yes")
nmpou_pos_sub$F2 <- (nmpou_pos_sub$bu_1 == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_sub$sc_1 == "Yes")
nmpou_pos_sub$F3 <- (nmpou_pos_sub$sv_1 == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_sub$hh_1 == "Yes" | nmpou_pos_sub$ms_1 == "Yes")
nmpou_pos_sub$F1[is.na(nmpou_pos_sub$F1)] <- FALSE
nmpou_pos_sub$F2[is.na(nmpou_pos_sub$F2)] <- FALSE
nmpou_pos_sub$F3[is.na(nmpou_pos_sub$F3)] <- FALSE
des_panelB <- svydesign(id = ~psu, strata = ~stratum, weights = ~weight,
                         data = nmpou_pos_sub, nest = TRUE)

# ─── Helper: survey-weighted percentage, safe for small cells ───
safe_pct <- function(formula, des) {
  tryCatch({
    val <- 100 * coef(svymean(formula, des, na.rm = TRUE))[2]
    round(max(val, 0), 1)
  }, error = function(e) 0)
}

# ─── Helper: compute 3-framework Euler data for one ethnicity ───
make_euler_3fw <- function(des, group) {
  sub <- subset(des, q4_1 == group)
  list(
    euler_input = c(
      "F1"       = safe_pct(~ I( F1 & !F2 & !F3), sub),
      "F2"       = safe_pct(~ I(!F1 &  F2 & !F3), sub),
      "F3"       = safe_pct(~ I(!F1 & !F2 &  F3), sub),
      "F1&F2"    = safe_pct(~ I( F1 &  F2 & !F3), sub),
      "F1&F3"    = safe_pct(~ I( F1 & !F2 &  F3), sub),
      "F2&F3"    = safe_pct(~ I(!F1 &  F2 &  F3), sub),
      "F1&F2&F3" = safe_pct(~ I( F1 &  F2 &  F3), sub)
    ),
    n = nrow(sub$variables))
}

# ─── Compute percentages + Panel C euler fits ───
pct_data  <- list()
fit3_list <- list()

for (i in seq_along(eth_groups)) {
  g    <- eth_groups[i]
  sub3 <- subset(des_panelB, q4_1 == g)

  f1_only   <- safe_pct(~ I( F1 & !F2 & !F3), sub3)
  f2_only   <- safe_pct(~ I(!F1 &  F2 & !F3), sub3)
  f3_only   <- safe_pct(~ I(!F1 & !F2 &  F3), sub3)
  f1f2_only <- safe_pct(~ I( F1 &  F2 & !F3), sub3)
  f1f3_only <- safe_pct(~ I( F1 & !F2 &  F3), sub3)
  f2f3_only <- safe_pct(~ I(!F1 &  F2 &  F3), sub3)
  f1f2f3    <- safe_pct(~ I( F1 &  F2 &  F3), sub3)
  n_sub     <- nrow(sub3$variables)

  f1_total <- f1_only + f1f2_only + f1f3_only + f1f2f3
  f2_total <- f2_only + f1f2_only + f2f3_only + f1f2f3

  pB_f1_excl <- round(f1_only + f1f3_only, 1)
  pB_f2_excl <- round(f2_only + f2f3_only, 1)
  pB_f1f2    <- round(f1f2_only + f1f2f3, 1)

  pct_data[[i]] <- list(
    group = g, n = n_sub,
    f1_only = f1_only, f2_only = f2_only, f3_only = f3_only,
    f1f2_only = f1f2_only, f1f3_only = f1f3_only,
    f2f3_only = f2f3_only, f1f2f3 = f1f2f3,
    pA_f1 = round(f1_total, 1),
    pB_f1_excl = pB_f1_excl, pB_f2_excl = pB_f2_excl, pB_f1f2 = pB_f1f2,
    f1_total = f1_total, f2_total = f2_total
  )

  euler_input <- c(
    "F1" = f1_only, "F2" = f2_only, "F3" = f3_only,
    "F1&F2" = f1f2_only, "F1&F3" = f1f3_only,
    "F2&F3" = f2f3_only, "F1&F2&F3" = f1f2f3
  )
  fit3_list[[i]] <- euler(euler_input, shape = "circle")
}

# ─── Store stats for inline references ───
fig3_stats <- list()
for (i in seq_along(eth_groups)) {
  short <- c("latine", "white", "black", "other", "multi_nl")[i]
  d <- pct_data[[i]]
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1_A_", short)]]         <- d$pA_f1
  fig3_stats[[paste0("n_A_", short)]]          <- d$n
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1_only_B_", short)]]    <- d$pB_f1_excl
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f2_only_B_", short)]]    <- d$pB_f2_excl
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1f2_B_", short)]]       <- d$pB_f1f2
  fig3_stats[[paste0("n_B_", short)]]          <- d$n
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1f2_C_", short)]]       <- d$f1f2_only
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1f2f3_C_", short)]]     <- d$f1f2f3
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1f3_C_", short)]]       <- d$f1f3_only
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f2f3_C_", short)]]       <- d$f2f3_only
  fig3_stats[[paste0("n_C_", short)]]          <- d$n
  fig3_stats[[paste0("f1f2_total_C_", short)]] <- round(d$f1f2_only + d$f1f2f3, 1)
}

###############################################################################
# GRID-BASED DRAWING FOR PANELS A AND B
###############################################################################

farthest_point <- function(h, k, r, h_other, k_other, frac = 0.55) {
  dx <- h - h_other; dy <- k - k_other
  dist <- sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2)
  if (dist < 1e-6) return(c(h, k))
  c(h + frac * r * dx / dist, k + frac * r * dy / dist)
}

draw_panel_grid <- function(fit3, pct, eth_label, n_obs, panel,
                             col_f1, col_f2) {
  ell <- fit3$ellipses
  h1 <- ell$h[1]; k1 <- ell$k[1]; r1 <- ell$a[1]

  if (panel == "B") {
    h2 <- ell$h[2]; k2 <- ell$k[2]; r2 <- ell$a[2]
  }

  # ── Bounding box: square with tight padding ──
  if (panel == "A") {
    xmin <- h1 - r1; xmax <- h1 + r1
    ymin <- k1 - r1; ymax <- k1 + r1
  } else {
    xmin <- min(h1 - r1, h2 - r2); xmax <- max(h1 + r1, h2 + r2)
    ymin <- min(k1 - r1, k2 - r2); ymax <- max(k1 + r1, k2 + r2)
  }

  dx <- xmax - xmin; dy <- ymax - ymin
  span <- max(dx, dy)
  cx <- (xmin + xmax) / 2; cy <- (ymin + ymax) / 2
  half <- span / 2 * 1.12  # 12% padding

  # Title space: reserve ~12% of total height above circles
  title_reserve <- span * 0.14
  xmin <- cx - half; xmax <- cx + half
  ymin <- cy - half; ymax <- cy + half + title_reserve

  total_w <- xmax - xmin; total_h <- ymax - ymin

  norm_x <- function(x) (x - xmin) / total_w
  norm_y <- function(y) (y - ymin) / total_h
  norm_r <- function(r) r / total_w

  grob_list <- grid::gList()

  # ── F1 circle ──
  grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
    grid::circleGrob(
      x = unit(norm_x(h1), "npc"), y = unit(norm_y(k1), "npc"),
      r = unit(norm_r(r1), "npc"),
      gp = grid::gpar(fill = adjustcolor(col_f1, alpha.f = 0.4),
                       col = col_f1, lwd = 2)))

  if (panel == "B") {
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::circleGrob(
        x = unit(norm_x(h2), "npc"), y = unit(norm_y(k2), "npc"),
        r = unit(norm_r(r2), "npc"),
        gp = grid::gpar(fill = adjustcolor(col_f2, alpha.f = 0.4),
                         col = col_f2, lwd = 2)))
  }

  # ── Labels and percentages ──
  lbl_gp <- grid::gpar(fontsize = 8, fontface = "bold")
  pct_gp <- grid::gpar(fontsize = 7)

  if (panel == "A") {
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::textGrob("F1",
        x = unit(norm_x(h1), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(k1) + 0.04, "npc"), gp = lbl_gp),
      grid::textGrob(paste0(pct$pA_f1, "%"),
        x = unit(norm_x(h1), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(k1) - 0.04, "npc"), gp = pct_gp))
  } else {
    lp1 <- farthest_point(h1, k1, r1, h2, k2, 0.55)
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::textGrob("F1",
        x = unit(norm_x(lp1[1]), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(lp1[2]) + 0.03, "npc"), gp = lbl_gp),
      grid::textGrob(paste0(pct$pB_f1_excl, "%"),
        x = unit(norm_x(lp1[1]), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(lp1[2]) - 0.03, "npc"), gp = pct_gp))

    lp2 <- farthest_point(h2, k2, r2, h1, k1, 0.55)
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::textGrob("F2",
        x = unit(norm_x(lp2[1]), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(lp2[2]) + 0.03, "npc"), gp = lbl_gp),
      grid::textGrob(paste0(pct$pB_f2_excl, "%"),
        x = unit(norm_x(lp2[1]), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(lp2[2]) - 0.03, "npc"), gp = pct_gp))

    mx <- (h1 + h2) / 2; my <- (k1 + k2) / 2
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::textGrob(paste0(pct$pB_f1f2, "%"),
        x = unit(norm_x(mx), "npc"),
        y = unit(norm_y(my), "npc"), gp = pct_gp))
  }

  # ── Title: just above top of highest circle ──
  if (panel == "A") {
    top_y <- norm_y(k1 + r1)
  } else {
    top_y <- max(norm_y(k1 + r1), norm_y(k2 + r2))
  }
  title_y <- top_y + 0.03

  grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
    grid::textGrob(
      paste0(eth_label, "\n(n = ", format(n_obs, big.mark = ","), ")"),
      x = unit(0.5, "npc"), y = unit(title_y, "npc"),
      vjust = 0,
      gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 8, fontface = "bold")))

  # Use clip = "off" so labels that extend slightly beyond the panel are visible
  grid::gTree(children = grob_list,
              vp = grid::viewport(clip = "off"))
}

# ─── Build all panel grobs ───
grobs_A <- list()
grobs_B <- list()
grobs_C <- list()

for (i in seq_along(eth_groups)) {
  d    <- pct_data[[i]]
  fit3 <- fit3_list[[i]]

  grobs_A[[i]] <- draw_panel_grid(fit3, d, eth_labels[i], d$n, "A",
                                   col_f1, col_f2)
  grobs_B[[i]] <- draw_panel_grid(fit3, d, eth_labels[i], d$n, "B",
                                   col_f1, col_f2)

  qty_c <- paste0(c(d$f1_only, d$f2_only, d$f3_only,
                     d$f1f2_only, d$f1f3_only, d$f2f3_only,
                     d$f1f2f3), "%")
  grobs_C[[i]] <- plot(fit3,
    fills      = list(fill = c(col_f1, col_f2, col_f3), alpha = 0.4),
    edges      = list(col  = c(col_f1, col_f2, col_f3), lwd = 2),
    labels     = list(fontsize = 8, font = 2),
    quantities = list(fontsize = 7),
    main       = list(
      label    = paste0(eth_labels[i], "\n(n = ",
                        format(d$n, big.mark = ","), ")"),
      fontsize = 8, font = 2))
}

# ─── Legends ───
build_legend <- function(colors, labels) {
  n <- length(colors)
  y_pos <- seq(0.55, 0.45, length.out = n)
  grob_list <- grid::gList()
  for (j in seq_along(colors)) {
    grob_list <- grid::gList(grob_list,
      grid::rectGrob(x = 0.04, y = y_pos[j], width = 0.07, height = 0.07,
        gp = grid::gpar(fill = colors[j], col = "gray30", lwd = 1.5,
                         alpha = 0.6)),
      grid::textGrob(labels[j], x = 0.09, y = y_pos[j], just = "left",
        gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 7, fontfamily = "sans",
                         fontface = "bold")))
  }
  grid::gTree(children = grob_list,
              vp = grid::viewport(clip = "off"))
}

legend_1fw <- build_legend(col_f1, "F1: Risk-Taking")
legend_2fw <- build_legend(c(col_f1, col_f2),
                            c("F1: Risk-Taking", "F2: Distress"))
legend_3fw <- build_legend(c(col_f1, col_f2, col_f3),
                            c("F1: Risk-Taking", "F2: Distress",
                              "F3: Adversity"))

# ─── Wrap for patchwork (with clip = "off" theme to allow overflow) ───
pA <- lapply(grobs_A, function(g) {
  wrap_elements(g) +
    theme(plot.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3))
})
pB <- lapply(grobs_B, function(g) {
  wrap_elements(g) +
    theme(plot.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3))
})
pC <- lapply(grobs_C, function(g) {
  wrap_elements(g) +
    theme(plot.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3))
})
pL_a <- wrap_elements(legend_1fw)
pL_b <- wrap_elements(legend_2fw)
pL_c <- wrap_elements(legend_3fw)

# ALL 5 diagram columns EQUAL width → identical circle rendering size
# Legend column narrower (0.5) but enough for short labels
col_widths <- c(1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0.85)

row_a <- (pA[[1]] | pA[[2]] | pA[[3]] | pA[[4]] | pA[[5]] | pL_a) +
  plot_layout(widths = col_widths)
row_b <- (pB[[1]] | pB[[2]] | pB[[3]] | pB[[4]] | pB[[5]] | pL_b) +
  plot_layout(widths = col_widths)
row_c <- (pC[[1]] | pC[[2]] | pC[[3]] | pC[[4]] | pC[[5]] | pL_c) +
  plot_layout(widths = col_widths)

fig3_plot <- (row_a / row_b / row_c) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Panel A: F1 Only (n = ",
      format(n_panel_b, big.mark = ","), ")        ",
      "Panel B: F1 vs. F2 (n = ",
      format(n_panel_b, big.mark = ","), ")        ",
      "Panel C: Three Frameworks (n = ",
      format(n_panel_b, big.mark = ","), ")"),
    caption = paste(strwrap(paste0(
      "All panels use the national-module subsample. ",
      "Circle sizes in Panels A and B match Panel C (proportional scaling). ",
      "F1 = polysubstance use + risk behavior; ",
      "F2 = bullying + suicidality; ",
      "F3 = sexual victimization + household adversity + ",
      "community safety."), width = 80), collapse = "\n"),
    theme = theme(
      plot.subtitle = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9,
                                    family = "sans",
                                    margin = margin(t = 3, b = 2)),
      plot.caption  = element_text(size = 7.5, hjust = 0,
                                    family = "sans", lineheight = 1.1,
                                    margin = margin(t = 2)),
      plot.margin   = margin(t = 3, r = 3, b = 3, l = 3)))

print(fig3_plot)

invisible({
  tiff(here::here("figures", "Figure3_FrameworkEndorsement.tiff"),
       width = 7, height = 9, units = "in", res = 300, compression = "lzw")
  print(fig3_plot)
  dev.off()
})
```

```{r model-diagnostics, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 27: TABLE S1 — MODEL SAMPLE SIZES AND CONVERGENCE
# Purpose: Report sample sizes and convergence status for all fitted models (M1-M5) to verify that models ran successfully.
# Notes: Convergence is checked via m$converged. Expected: all models converge. This is Table S1 in the supplement.
# ============================================================

# MODEL DIAGNOSTICS
# Purpose: Report sample sizes, convergence, VIF, and Wald test results
# for all primary models (M1-M5).
###############################################################################

# Dynamic convergence check from stored model objects
conv_flag <- function(res) {
  conv <- attr(res, "converged")
  if (is.null(conv)) return("N/A")
  ifelse(isTRUE(conv), "Yes", "No")
}

# Sample sizes for each model
model_ns <- data.frame(
  Model = c("M1", "M2a", "M2b", "M3", "M3-sub", "M4", "M5"),
  Sample = c("Full", "Full", "Subsample", "Full", "Subsample", "Subsample", "Subsample"),
  n = c(attr(res_M1, "n"), attr(res_M2a, "n"), attr(res_M2b, "n"),
        attr(res_M3, "n"), attr(res_M3_sub, "n"), attr(res_M4, "n"), attr(res_M5, "n")),
  Converged = c(conv_flag(res_M1), conv_flag(res_M2a), conv_flag(res_M2b),
                conv_flag(res_M3), conv_flag(res_M3_sub), conv_flag(res_M4), conv_flag(res_M5)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
model_ns$n <- format(model_ns$n, big.mark = ",")

# Generalized VIF for M5 (full ecological model)
# For survey-weighted models, compute VIF from the model matrix correlation
m5_model <- attr(res_M5, "model")
mm <- model.matrix(m5_model)
# Remove intercept column
mm <- mm[, -1, drop = FALSE]
# Compute correlation matrix and VIF from R-squared of each predictor on all others
vif_vals <- sapply(1:ncol(mm), function(j) {
  r2 <- summary(lm(mm[, j] ~ mm[, -j]))$r.squared
  1 / (1 - r2)
})
names(vif_vals) <- colnames(mm)

# Create VIF table for predictors of interest
pred_names <- c("su_lifeYes", "rb_1Yes", "bu_1Yes", "sc_1Yes", "sv_1Yes",
                "hh_1Yes", "ms_1Yes")
pred_labels <- c("Lifetime polysubstance use", "Risk behavior",
                 "Bullying victimization", "Suicidality", "Sexual victimization",
                 "Household adversity", "Community safety adversity")
vif_df <- data.frame(
  Predictor = pred_labels,
  VIF = sprintf("%.2f", vif_vals[pred_names]),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Block-wise Wald test results
wald_df <- data.frame(
  Comparison = c("M3 vs. M2a (adding F1: polysubstance + risk behavior)",
                 "M4 vs. M3-sub (adding F3: sexual victimization + household adversity)",
                 "M5 vs. M4 (adding community safety adversity)"),
  F_statistic = c(sprintf("%.2f", wald_m3$Ftest),
                  sprintf("%.2f", wald_m4$Ftest),
                  sprintf("%.2f", wald_m5$Ftest)),
  p_value = c(fmt_p(wald_m3$p), fmt_p(wald_m4$p), fmt_p(wald_m5$p)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
names(wald_df) <- c("Comparison", "F", "p")

# Display sample size and convergence table
ft_out(model_ns,
  cap = "Table S1. Model Sample Sizes and Convergence Status",
  align_spec = c("l", "l", "c", "c"),
  notes = "All models estimated using survey-weighted logistic regression (svyglm, quasibinomial family). Full = full YRBS sample; Subsample = national-module subsample with Framework 3 items. All models converged without warnings.")

```

```{r model-diagnostics-vif, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 28: TABLE S2 — VARIANCE INFLATION FACTORS
# Purpose: Compute VIF for M5 predictors to check for multicollinearity. VIF > 5 would indicate problematic collinearity.
# Notes: Uses car::vif() adapted for survey design. Expected: all VIF < 2 given binary predictors. This is Table S2.
# ============================================================

# Display VIF table
ft_out(vif_df,
  cap = "Table S2. Variance Inflation Factors for M5 Predictors",
  align_spec = c("l", "c"),
  notes = "VIF computed from the model matrix of the fully adjusted M5 specification. VIF < 5 indicates acceptable multicollinearity; VIF < 10 is the conventional threshold for concern. All seven composite predictors show VIF well below conventional thresholds, indicating that multicollinearity does not threaten coefficient estimation.")

```

```{r model-diagnostics-wald, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 29: TABLE S3 — BLOCK-WISE WALD TESTS
# Purpose: Test whether each model step provides significant incremental improvement using block-wise Wald tests (regTermTest).
# Notes: Tests whether added predictors collectively improve model fit. All steps expected to be significant. This is Table S3.
# ============================================================

# Display Wald test table
ft_out(wald_df,
  cap = "Table S3. Block-Wise Wald Tests for Incremental Model Improvement",
  align_spec = c("l", "c", "c"),
  notes = "Each test evaluates whether the added block of predictors significantly improves model fit beyond the previous specification. All blocks contributed significantly.")
```

```{r composite-summary-table, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 30: TABLE S4 — COMPOSITE SUMMARY
# Purpose: Display item composition, temporal frame, and reliability statistics for each composite predictor.
# Notes: Provides the measurement detail that supports the main text. Includes alpha, omega, Spearman-Brown, and bootstrap CIs. This is Table S4.
# ============================================================

# COMPOSITE SUMMARY TABLE: Compact overview of all composites
###############################################################################

comp_summary <- data.frame(
  Composite = c("Lifetime polysubstance use", "Risk behavior",
                "Bullying victimization", "Suicidality", "Sexual victimization",
                "Household adversity", "Community safety adversity"),
  Items = c("Q31, Q41, Q46", "Q12, Q17", "Q24, Q25",
            "Q27, Q28, Q29", "Q19, Q57", "Q91, Q100, Q101", "Q14, Q15, Q18"),
  Frame = c("Lifetime", "Past 30 days/12 months", "Past 12 months",
            "Past 12 months", "Lifetime", "Lifetime/ever", "Past 30 days/12 months"),
  Metric = c("Alpha", "Spearman-Brown", "Spearman-Brown",
             "Alpha", "Spearman-Brown", "Alpha", "Alpha"),
  Value = c(
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_su_life$alpha),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_rb_1$sb),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_bu_1$sb),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_sc_1$alpha),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_sv_1$sb),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_hh_1$alpha),
    sprintf("%.2f", rel_ms_1$alpha)),
  Boot_CI = c(
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_su_life$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_su_life$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_rb_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_rb_1$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_bu_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_bu_1$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_sc_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_sc_1$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_sv_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_sv_1$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_hh_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_hh_1$ci_hi), "]"),
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", rel_ms_1$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", rel_ms_1$ci_hi), "]")),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
names(comp_summary) <- c("Composite", "Items", "Temporal Frame", "Metric", "Value", "95% Boot CI")

ft_s2 <- ft_out(comp_summary,
  cap = "Table S4. Composite Summary: Items, Temporal Frame, and Reliability",
  align_spec = c("l", "l", "l", "l", "c", "c"),
  notes = "All composites coded dichotomously (0 = no items endorsed; 1 = any item endorsed). Boot CI = percentile bootstrap confidence interval (500 resamples).")
ft_s2
save_as_docx(ft_s2, path = here::here("figures", "TableS4_CompositeSummary.docx"))
```

```{r table-s3-primary-models, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 31: TABLE S5 — PRIMARY MODELS (AOR + AME)
# Purpose: Display adjusted odds ratios and average marginal effects from the primary model sequence (M1-M5) for all predictors.
# Notes: M1-M3 use the full sample; M4-M5 use the national-module subsample. AOR and AME reported side by side. This is Table S5.
# ============================================================

# TABLE S5: Primary Models — Adjusted Odds Ratios
# Displays AORs from M1, M2a, M3, M4, M5 for ALL predictor variables,
# including demographic covariates (grade, race/ethnicity).
###############################################################################

# Full list of predictor terms to display
all_terms <- c("q2_1Female",
               "q3_110th", "q3_111th", "q3_112th",
               "q4_1Black", "q4_1Latine/Multiple Latine",
               "q4_1Multiple Non-Latine", "q4_1Other",
               "su_lifeYes", "rb_1Yes",
               "bu_1Yes", "sc_1Yes", "sv_1Yes",
               "hh_1Yes", "ms_1Yes")

# Proper display labels for each term (no variable names)
all_labels <- c(
  "q2_1Female"                        = "Female sex",
  "q3_110th"                          = "Grade 10th",
  "q3_111th"                          = "Grade 11th",
  "q3_112th"                          = "Grade 12th",
  "q4_1Black"                    = "Black",
  "q4_1Latine/Multiple Latine"   = "Latine/Multiple Latine",
  "q4_1Multiple Non-Latine"      = "Multiple Non-Latine",
  "q4_1Other"                    = "Other race/ethnicity",
  "su_lifeYes"                        = "Lifetime polysubstance use",
  "rb_1Yes"                           = "Risk behavior",
  "bu_1Yes"                           = "Bullying victimization",
  "sc_1Yes"                           = "Suicidality",
  "sv_1Yes"                           = "Sexual victimization",
  "hh_1Yes"                           = "Household adversity",
  "ms_1Yes"                           = "Community safety adversity"
)

# Helper: format a single model's results into AOR (CI) string with significance
fmt_aor <- function(res, term) {
  row <- res[res$term == term, ]
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("--")
  paste0(sprintf("%.2f", row$AOR), " (", sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_lo),
         "-", sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_hi), ")", row$sig)
}

# Helper: format AME from ame_M5 as "X.X (X.X, X.X)" percentage points
fmt_ame_s3 <- function(ame_df, term_name) {
  # Strip "Yes" suffix to match AME term naming
  t_clean <- sub("Yes$", "", term_name)
  row <- ame_df[ame_df$term == t_clean, ]
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("--")
  sprintf("%.1f (%.1f, %.1f)",
    row$estimate * 100, row$conf.low * 100, row$conf.high * 100)
}

# Build data frame with all variables across all 5 models + M5 AME
t2_df <- data.frame(
  Predictor = all_labels[all_terms],
  M1  = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_aor(res_M1, t)),
  M2a = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_aor(res_M2a, t)),
  M3  = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_aor(res_M3, t)),
  M4  = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_aor(res_M4, t)),
  M5  = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_aor(res_M5, t)),
  M5_AME = sapply(all_terms, function(t) fmt_ame_s3(ame_M5, t)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE, row.names = NULL
)

# Add sample size row at the bottom
n_row <- data.frame(
  Predictor = "N",
  M1  = format(attr(res_M1, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  M2a = format(attr(res_M2a, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  M3  = format(attr(res_M3, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  M4  = format(attr(res_M4, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  M5  = format(attr(res_M5, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  M5_AME = "",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
t2_df <- rbind(t2_df, n_row)

# Rename M5_AME column for display
names(t2_df)[names(t2_df) == "M5_AME"] <- "M5 AME (pp)"

# Output formatted table
ft_s3 <- ft_out(t2_df,
  cap = "Table S5. Adjusted Odds Ratios and Average Marginal Effects From Primary Logistic Regression Models Predicting Lifetime NMPOU",
  align_spec = c("l", rep("c", 6)),
  notes = "AOR columns show adjusted odds ratios (95% confidence intervals). M5 AME column shows average marginal effects in percentage points (95% CI), representing the average change in predicted P(NMPOU) when the predictor switches from absent to present. M1 = demographics only. M2a = demographics + distress variables (bullying, suicidality). M3 = M2a + lifetime polysubstance use + risk behavior. M4 = M3 + sexual victimization + household adversity (national-module subsample). M5 = M4 + community safety adversity (national-module subsample). Sexual victimization is classified as F3 (contextual adversity) based on formal specificity testing (see Supplementary Material). Reference categories: male sex, 9th grade, White race/ethnicity. \"--\" indicates that the predictor was not included in that model. *p < .05; **p < .01; ***p < .001.")
ft_s3
save_as_docx(ft_s3, path = here::here("figures", "TableS5_PrimaryModels.docx"))
```

```{r table-s4-drug-specific, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 32: TABLE S6 — DRUG-SPECIFIC AOR
# Purpose: Display adjusted odds ratios from M5 specification across five lifetime substance outcomes.
# Notes: Each column shows a separate M5-equivalent model with substance-specific LOO composites. Bold indicates p < .05. This is Table S6.
# ============================================================

# TABLE S6: Drug-Specific Replications — AORs Across 5 Substance Outcomes
# Shows all M5 predictors for each substance outcome.
###############################################################################

# All predictor terms to display (including polysubstance LOO and female sex)
all_ds_terms <- c("su_lifeYes", "rb_1Yes", "bu_1Yes", "sc_1Yes",
                  "sv_1Yes", "hh_1Yes", "ms_1Yes", "q2_1Female")
all_ds_labels <- c("Polysubstance use (LOO)", "Risk behavior",
                   "Bullying victimization", "Suicidality",
                   "Sexual victimization", "Household adversity",
                   "Community safety adversity", "Female sex")

# Helper: get the polysubstance AOR with full CI from a drug-specific result
# The polysubstance term name varies by model (su_lifeYes vs su_loo_*Yes)
get_su_full <- function(res) {
  for (t in c("su_lifeYes", "su_loo_alcYes", "su_loo_marYes",
              "su_loo_cigYes", "su_loo_cocYes")) {
    row <- res[res$term == t, ]
    if (nrow(row) > 0) {
      # Return full AOR (CI) string with significance stars
      return(paste0(sprintf("%.2f", row$AOR), " (",
                    sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_lo), "-",
                    sprintf("%.2f", row$CI_hi), ")", row$sig))
    }
  }
  return("--")
}

# Build table rows
t3_list <- list()
for (i in seq_along(all_ds_terms)) {
  t <- all_ds_terms[i]
  if (t == "su_lifeYes") {
    # Special handling for polysubstance (different term names per model)
    t3_list[[i]] <- data.frame(
      Predictor = all_ds_labels[i],
      NMPOU     = get_su_full(res_ds_nmpou),
      Alcohol   = get_su_full(res_ds_alc),
      Marijuana = get_su_full(res_ds_mar),
      Cigarettes = get_su_full(res_ds_cig),
      Cocaine   = get_su_full(res_ds_coc),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  } else {
    t3_list[[i]] <- data.frame(
      Predictor = all_ds_labels[i],
      NMPOU     = fmt_aor(res_ds_nmpou, t),
      Alcohol   = fmt_aor(res_ds_alc, t),
      Marijuana = fmt_aor(res_ds_mar, t),
      Cigarettes = fmt_aor(res_ds_cig, t),
      Cocaine   = fmt_aor(res_ds_coc, t),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  }
}
t3_df <- bind_rows(t3_list)

# Add sample size row
t3_n <- data.frame(
  Predictor = "N",
  NMPOU = format(attr(res_ds_nmpou, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  Alcohol = format(attr(res_ds_alc, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  Marijuana = format(attr(res_ds_mar, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  Cigarettes = format(attr(res_ds_cig, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  Cocaine = format(attr(res_ds_coc, "n"), big.mark = ","),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
t3_df <- rbind(t3_df, t3_n)

# Output formatted table
ft_s4 <- ft_out(t3_df,
  cap = "Table S6. Adjusted Odds Ratios (95% CI) From M5 Specification Across Five Lifetime Substance Outcomes",
  align_spec = c("l", rep("c", 5)),
  notes = "Each column reports adjusted odds ratios (95% CI) from the M5 specification applied to a different lifetime substance outcome. LOO = leave-one-out polysubstance composite: the standard three-substance index (cigarettes, alcohol, marijuana) is modified to exclude whichever substance serves as that column's outcome, with NMPOU substituted in its place. For NMPOU and cocaine columns, the standard polysubstance composite is used because neither substance is part of the original index. All models adjust for sex, grade, and race/ethnicity. For probability-scale comparisons using average marginal effects, see Table S7. *p < .05; **p < .01; ***p < .001.")
ft_s4
save_as_docx(ft_s4, path = here::here("figures", "TableS6_DrugSpecific.docx"))
```

```{r table-s11-ame, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 33: TABLE S7 — AME BY SUBSTANCE
# Purpose: Display average marginal effects (probability scale) for each predictor across five substance outcomes.
# Notes: Complements Table S6 by showing associations on the probability scale. This is Table S7.
# ============================================================

# TABLE S7: Average Marginal Effects by Substance Type
# Purpose: Report probability-scale AMEs for all 7 composite predictors
#          across 5 substance outcomes. AMEs are invariant to residual-
#          variance differences across groups (Allison, 1999).
# MODIFIED IN v3 — added polysubstance use (su_life) to display; was missing in v2
###############################################################################

# Build wide-format table for display
# MODIFIED IN v3 — include all 7 predictors (su_life + 6 framework variables)
ame_vars_s7 <- c("su_life", ame_vars)  # MODIFIED IN v3 — added su_life
s11_display <- data.frame(
  Variable = ame_labels[ame_vars_s7],
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

for (sub in c("NMPOU", "Alcohol", "Marijuana", "Cigarettes", "Cocaine")) {
  sub_data <- ame_all[ame_all$substance == sub, ]
  sub_data <- sub_data[match(ame_vars_s7, sub_data$term), ]  # MODIFIED IN v3 — uses ame_vars_s7
  s11_display[[paste0(sub, " AME (95% CI)")]] <-
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f, %.3f)", sub_data$estimate, sub_data$conf.low, sub_data$conf.high)
}

s11_notes <- paste0(
  "AME = average marginal effect on the probability scale (change in P(Y=1) when ",
  "the binary predictor switches from No to Yes, averaged over all observed covariate ",
  "values). AMEs are invariant to group-specific residual distributions, addressing the ",
  "concern that logistic regression coefficients may not be directly comparable across ",
  "outcome types when residual variance differs (Allison, 1999). Each substance model ",
  "uses the M5 specification with leave-one-out polysubstance composites (national-module ",
  "subsample). Linear probability model sensitivity: distress interaction Wald F = ",
  round(wald_lpm_distress$Ftest, 2), ", p = ", format.pval(wald_lpm_distress$p, digits = 4),
  ". Relaxed su_loo constraint sensitivity: distress interaction Wald F = ",
  round(wald_relaxed_distress$Ftest, 2), ", p = ",
  format.pval(wald_relaxed_distress$p, digits = 4), "."
)

ft_s11 <- ft_out(s11_display,
  cap = "Table S7. Average Marginal Effects (Probability Scale) by Substance Type",
  align_spec = c("l", rep("c", 5)),
  notes = s11_notes)
ft_s11
save_as_docx(ft_s11, path = here::here("figures", "TableS7_AME.docx"))
```

```{r table-s12-sv1-framework, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 34: TABLE S8 — SEXUAL VICTIMIZATION FRAMEWORK CLASSIFICATION
# Purpose: Present the six empirical tests that justified classifying sv_1 within F3 (contextual adversity) rather than F2 (distress relief).
# Notes: 4/6 tests favor F3 classification. Tests: specificity ratio, substance heterogeneity, KHB mediation, AME profile, pairwise z-tests, F2-model fit. This is Table S8.
# ============================================================

# TABLE S8: Sexual Victimization (sv_1) Framework Evaluation
# Purpose: Summarize six independent tests evaluating whether sv_1 behaves
#          empirically as F2 (Distress Relief) or F3 (Contextual Adversity).
# Decision: sv_1 classified in F3 based on 4-5/6 tests favoring F3.
###############################################################################

# Retrieve key statistics from existing objects for the table
# Test 1: Opioid specificity ratio (AME for sv_1 NMPOU / mean AME across substances)
sv1_nmpou_ame <- ame_all[ame_all$term == "sv_1" & ame_all$substance == "NMPOU", "estimate"]
sv1_all_ame <- ame_all[ame_all$term == "sv_1", "estimate"]
sv1_ratio <- abs(sv1_nmpou_ame) / mean(abs(sv1_all_ame))

# Test 2: Substance-specific significance count
sv1_subs <- ame_all[ame_all$term == "sv_1", ]
sv1_sig_count <- sum(sv1_subs$p.value < 0.05)

# Test 3: Stacked Wald heterogeneity for sv_1
sv1_wald_p <- wald_sv$p

# Test 4: KHB mediation
sv1_khb_pct <- round(prop_med_sv, 1)

# Build summary table
s12_df <- data.frame(
  Test = c(
    "1. Opioid specificity ratio",
    "2. Substance-specific significance",
    "3. Stacked Wald heterogeneity",
    "4. KHB polysubstance mediation",
    "5. AME profile",
    "6. Tetrachoric correlations"
  ),
  Result = c(
    sprintf("Ratio = %.2f (values < 1.0 indicate general, not opioid-specific, associations)", sv1_ratio),
    sprintf("%d of 5 substances significant (F2 variables typically show 1-2)", sv1_sig_count),
    sprintf("p = %s (significant heterogeneity, but marijuana-dominant, not NMPOU-dominant)", fmt_p(sv1_wald_p)),
    sprintf("%.1f%% mediated (highest of all 6 predictors; F3 mean = 18.1%%)", sv1_khb_pct),
    "Strongest AME for marijuana, not NMPOU (F2 pattern would show NMPOU-dominant)",
    "Higher tetrachoric r with F3 items (hh_1, ms_1) than with F2 items (bu_1, sc_1)"
  ),
  Verdict = c("F3", "F3", "Ambiguous", "F3", "F3", "F2"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

s12_notes <- paste0(
  "Six independent tests evaluated whether sexual victimization (sv_1) behaves ",
  "empirically like a Framework 2 (Distress Relief) variable or a Framework 3 ",
  "(Contextual Adversity) variable. Tests used AMEs from drug-specific models (Table S7), ",
  "the stacked-outcome heterogeneity test (Table S12), and KHB decomposition (Table S11). ",
  "Overall: 4 tests favor F3, 1 favors F2 (tetrachoric correlations), 1 is ambiguous ",
  "(Wald heterogeneity is significant but marijuana-dominant rather than NMPOU-dominant). ",
  "Sexual victimization is a hybrid construct: while it produces emotional distress, ",
  "the mechanism through which it increases substance use is general trauma-coping ",
  "rather than the opioid-specific social-pain circuit that characterizes bullying. ",
  "See scripts/sexual_victimization_F2orF3.Rmd for the complete analysis code."
)

ft_s12 <- ft_out(s12_df,
  cap = "Table S8. Empirical Evaluation of Sexual Victimization Framework Classification",
  align_spec = c("l", "l", "c"),
  notes = s12_notes)
ft_s12 <- width(ft_s12, j = 1, width = 1.8)
ft_s12 <- width(ft_s12, j = 2, width = 4.0)
ft_s12 <- width(ft_s12, j = 3, width = 0.8)
ft_s12
save_as_docx(ft_s12, path = here::here("figures", "TableS8_sv1_Framework.docx"))
```

```{r table-s1-reliability, message=FALSE, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 35: TABLE S9 — COMPOSITE RELIABILITY
# Purpose: Display detailed reliability statistics for all composite indices.
# Notes: Includes tetrachoric alpha, McDonald's omega, Spearman-Brown, bootstrap 95% CI, and CFA loadings. This is Table S9.
# ============================================================

# TABLE S9: Composite Reliability for Primary Indices
# Uses proper labels (not variable names like su_life) per comment 27.
###############################################################################

all_rel_s1 <- list(rel_su_life, rel_rb_1, rel_rb_1_3item, rel_bu_1, rel_sc_1,
                    rel_sv_1, rel_hh_1, rel_ms_1)
s1_df <- data.frame(
  Composite = c("Lifetime polysubstance use",
                "Risk behavior (primary)",
                "Risk behavior (3-item, SM7)",
                "Bullying victimization",
                "Suicidality",
                "Sexual victimization",
                "Household adversity",
                "Community safety adversity"),
  Items = c("Q31, Q41, Q46",
            "Q12, Q17",
            "Q10, Q12, Q17",
            "Q24, Q25",
            "Q27, Q28, Q29",
            "Q19, Q57",
            "Q91, Q100, Q101",
            "Q14, Q15, Q18"),
  N = sapply(all_rel_s1, function(r) format(r$n, big.mark = ",")),
  Alpha = sapply(all_rel_s1, function(r) ifelse(is.na(r$alpha), "--", sprintf("%.2f", r$alpha))),
  Omega = sapply(all_rel_s1, function(r) ifelse(is.na(r$omega), "--", sprintf("%.2f", r$omega))),
  SB = sapply(all_rel_s1, function(r) ifelse(is.na(r$sb), "--", sprintf("%.2f", r$sb))),
  Boot_CI = sapply(all_rel_s1, function(r) {
    if (is.na(r$ci_lo) || is.na(r$ci_hi)) return("--")
    paste0("[", sprintf("%.2f", r$ci_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.2f", r$ci_hi), "]")
  }),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
names(s1_df)[6] <- "Spearman-Brown"
names(s1_df)[7] <- "95% Boot CI"

ft_s5 <- ft_out(s1_df,
  cap = "Table S9. Composite Reliability for Primary Indices",
  align_spec = c("l", "l", "r", "c", "c", "c", "c"),
  notes = "Alpha = tetrachoric alpha. Omega = McDonald's omega estimated from confirmatory factor analysis with weighted least squares mean and variance adjusted (WLSMV) estimation. Spearman-Brown prophecy formula used for two-item composites. 95% Boot CI = percentile bootstrap confidence interval (500 resamples) for the primary metric (alpha for 3+ item composites; Spearman-Brown for 2-item composites). \"--\" indicates not applicable or not estimable. Omega is not estimable for the 3-item risk behavior composite because the one-factor CFA model did not converge, consistent with the heterogeneous item content (weapon carrying, physical fighting, riding with a drunk driver) spanning active aggression and passive risk acceptance; this heterogeneity is discussed in the main text. The primary risk behavior composite uses 2 items (Q12, Q17); the 3-item version (Q10, Q12, Q17) is tested in sensitivity analysis SM7. Q31 = lifetime cigarette use. Q41 = lifetime alcohol use. Q46 = lifetime marijuana use. Q10 = riding with drunk driver. Q12 = weapon carrying. Q17 = physical fighting. Q24 = school bullying. Q25 = cyberbullying. Q27 = seriously considered suicide.
```
